The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Immensely Pleasing Joachimsdaler with Lovely Toning
13060
DENMARK. Joachimsdaler (Taler), 1537. Copenhagen Mint. Christian III. NGC EF-40. Dav-8226; Galster-102; 
Sieg-19; Schou-12; Bruun-4277. Weight: 28.94 gms. Mintmaster: Reynold Junge. A exquisite example of this early Dan-
ish Taler struck the year after the Danish Reformation. 
The model for this type was the silver coins minted in the German city of Joachimsthal by the counts of Schlick.
€7,000-€9,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 297 in 
his collection.

========== PAGE 55 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 29
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Johan Peter Samuel Goldschmidt, who as a coin collector is best known by 
the surname Guildal, which he acquired in 1906, was born in Copenhagen in 
1855, the son of a shipbroker. After completing an apprenticeship in a whole-
saler’s company, he traveled to both England and Germany to study dyeing 
technology, so that on his return he was qualified to enter his uncle’s dye -
ing company, Goldschmidt and Nordholm. Together with the Swede Anders 
Nordholm, Johan Guildal took over the management as early as 1880; when 
the company changed to a limited company in 1906, he continued as director 
until 1919. At the end of the 19th 
century and the beginning of the 
20th, Guildal was a central figure 
within Danish industry and crafts-
manship and held board positions 
in many important companies 
such as Handelsbanken, The Dan-
ish Coal Company, Danish Lloyd, 
The Danish-Russian Steamship 
Company, Atlas Machine Factory 
and Portland Cement Factory.
In 1899, the same year he was awarded the Order of the Dannebrog, Guil-
dal participated in the nine-month long voyage to East Asia on board the 
protected cruiser, Valkyrien (“The Valkyrie”), under the command of the 
king’s youngest son, Prince Valdemar.  The trip had been advocated ex-
pressly by the Danish parliament at the urging of Danish business inter -
ests. On the way visits were made to both the Siamese king Chulalongkorn 
and the Japanese emperor Meiji.
Guildal was also a prominent art collector, and his collection of old master prints was sold by Winkel & Magnus -
sen in 1931. Prints from his collection (easily recognizable from the small ink stamp verso, Lugt 1110a) can now be 
found in museums all over the world, including at the Metropolitan Museum in New Y ork and the Rijksmuseum in 
Amsterdam.
His main passion, however, was his coin collection. He was one of the founding fathers of the first Danish society for 
coin collectors formed in 1885, where L. E. Bruun was also a member. From 1914 through 1916 he acted as chair -
man of the still existing Numismatisk Forening (Danish Numismatic Society). Unlike L. E. Bruun, he didn’t collect 
medieval coins, but instead focused on the issues struck by the House of Oldenburg, which ruled Denmark from 
1448 to 1863.
Given his interest in the graphic arts it was only natural that he also collected paper money. Whereas this collection 
of 562 pieces is today a part of the collection at the National Museum of Denmark, Guildal decided to sell his coin 
collection in its entirety to L. E. Bruun in June 1918. Guildal was not a stranger to the idea of trading large collections 
en bloc, as he had himself acquired all Major General Carl Thorvald Jørgensen’s (1819-1902) formidable collection 
of Danish and Norwegian coins in 1901.
Shortly before his death on December 21, 1920, Guildal’s collection was published as vol. III of “Numismatisk 
Forenings Medlemsblad” and cataloged by Hans Henrik Schou. 
J.G. Guildal, Danish Royal Library.
Royal Danish Order of the 
Dannebrog, Grand Cross Star.
J. G. GUILDAL
(1850-1920)

13061
DENMARK. 1/2 Joachimsdaler (1/2 Taler), 1537. Copenhagen Mint. Christian III. NGC AU Details— Environ -
mental Damage. Galster-103; Sieg-17; Schou-17; Bruun-4279. Weight: 14.11 gms. The 1/2 Taler is far rarer than the 
Taler with only a handful of examples in private hands.
€9,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
13062
DENMARK. 2 Skilling, 1545. Copenhagen Mint. Christian III. NGC AU-53. Hede-9; Sieg-33; Schou-9; Bruun-4295. 
Weight: 3.60 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. Outstanding example of this excessively rare one-year type with a unique 
obverse design that harks back to pre-monetary reform reverses issued in Copenhagen, Roskilde, Ribe and Aarhus in 
1536. 
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Frederik Chr. Bech Collection (Copenhagen - 1906) Lot # 41.

========== PAGE 57 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 31
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Wholesome and Extremely Rare One Y ear Type Silver Gulden
13063
DENMARK. Silver Gulden (Taler), 1545. Flensburg Mint. Christian III. NGC VF-35. Dav-8236; Hede-16; Sieg-46; 
Schou-2; Bruun-4300. Weight: 28.69 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Degener. With just a hair-line split in the flan to detract 
from a formidable eye-appeal this is a wonderfully desirable example of this excessively rare one-year type. The portrait 
is clearly inspired by the works of the German artist Jacob Binck who was appointed painter to king Christian, and might 
in fact be his work. 
The Royal Flensburg mint was established in 1544 as the former mint in Gottorp had passed to the king’s half-brother. It was 
in use for only 27 years, from 1544 until 1571, when it was turned into a bell foundry.
€20,000-€30,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

Page 32 Stack’s Bowers Galleries

Unique, Attractive and Highly Important
13064
DENMARK. 1/2 Silver Gulden, 1545. Flensburg Mint. Christian III. NGC AU Details—Tooled. Hede-17A; Sieg-
44.1; Schou-3; Bruun-4301. Weight: 14.50 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Degener. Although lightly tooled this can only be 
described as a phenomenal example, and arguably one of the most important coins in the whole collection. Only two 
examples of the Half Taler of this overall type are known, one in the National Museum of Copenhagen (holed) and the 
present unique and markedly different variant where the reverse shield is crowned.
€30,000-€50,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 60 ==========

Page 34 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Nicely Struck on a Broad Flan
13068
DENMARK. Gold Gulden (Ungersk Gylden) Klippe, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-61. Fr-21; 
Hede-1; Sieg-27; Schou-1; Bruun-4418. Weight: 3.76 gms. Mintmaster: Johan of Vilna (Hans de Willers). A lovely Mint 
State Klippe, with bright luster and limited handling.
This klippe was struck in a number of 15,504 specimens to finance expenses in connection with the sudden outbreak of the 
Northern Seven Years’ War. They where rather crudely minted on unevenly cut flans and it is extremely rare to find an ex-
ample this nicely centered and struck on a broad flan.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 376 in 
his collection.
    
13069
DENMARK. Speciedaler / 3 Mark, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC EF Details—Reverse Graffiti. Dav-
8228; Hede-8B; Sieg-21; Schou-5; Bruun-4422. Weight: 28.76 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. A contemporary owner’s 
mark in the lower reverse field doesn’t detract from the present example being an exceptionally handsome piece. This 
one-year type that quite unusually bears the date in Roman numerals (LXIII) has one of the highest silver fineness 
(0.937) of all Danish coins minted since the early medieval period. 
€1,200-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 61 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 35
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
13070
DENMARK. Mark, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-64. Hede-9B; Sieg-15.2; Schou-77; Bruun-4443. 
Weight: 9.57 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. More than 100 different variations are known of this interesting issue as it 
was in fact minted with the same date between 1563 and 1576. Although the Mark of 1563 is still very common among 
collectors in ordinary condition, it is however a true rarity in a spectacular condition as the present. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Extremely Rare and Lustrous Klippe
13071
DENMARK. Gold Krone (Golt Krone) Klippe, 1564. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU Details—Scratches. 
Fr-23; Hede-5; Sieg-26; Schou-2; Bruun-4487. Weight:3.39 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Köppelin. Some light scratches but 
exceptionally well-struck and lustrous and excessively rare with only a few in private hands. 
This interesting klippe, struck during the Northern Seven Years War, was minted in the naval anchor forge on Bremerholm 
in only 600 specimens. The mintmaster, Hans Köppelin was transferred to Flensburg in 1566 where he acted until 1570.
€15,000-€20,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Frederik Chr. Bech Collection (Copenhagen - 1906) Lot # 81.
Ex: Alfred Benzon Collection (Copenhagen - 1887) Lot # 152.

Exceedingly Rare with Lovely Russet Toning
13072
DENMARK. Gold Gulden (Rhinsk Gylden) Klippe, 1564. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU-58. Fr-24; Hede-
6; Sieg-24; Schou-3; Bruun-4488. Weight: 3.13 gms. An alluring golden-orange toning further enhances the already 
sharply struck details. Originally minted by Hans Köppelin in only 995 examples and today of the utmost rarity.
Presumably struck in the naval anchor forge on Bremerholm next to the royal castle. In 1619 the forge was turned into Hol-
mens Church, which still stands today.
€18,000-€22,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
13073
DENMARK. 2 Mark Klippe, 1564. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik ll. NGC AU-55. Hede-13; Sieg-20; Schou-4; Bru -
un-4489. 15.51 gms. A nicely centered and lustrous example of a very rare date. 
These primitive and base coins, which were minted during the Northern Seven Years’ War, were so badly regarded by the 
population that the king had to erect gallows around the country in order to threaten the death penalty if anyone refused to 
accept the king’s coin at face value. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

Mint State Renaissance Portrait with Charming Toning
13074
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1572. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-61. Dav-8229; Hede-17; Sieg-22; Schou-8; 
Aagaard & Märcher-72.10; Bruun-4523. Weight: 28.85 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Delhusen. Engraver: Cort Dellhusen. 
Lovely iridescent toning and an exquisite example of numismatic Renaissance portraiture. 
This beautiful type, which for the first time carries the three crowns of Sweden in the Danish royal arms, was struck from 
silver paid by the Swedish king to reacquire the fortress, Elfsborg, which had been conquered by the Danes during the North-
ern Seven Years War.
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 64 ==========

Page 38 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Imposing and Fascinating Portugaloser  
Only Two in Private Hands
13076
DENMARK. Portugaloser (10 Ducats), 1591. Haderslev Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Mount Removed. 
Fr-62; Hede-1; Sieg-159; Harck-2.1; Schou-3; Bruun-4595. Weight: 34.61 gms. Mintmaster: Andreas Metzner. An awe-
inspiring example with traces of luster. The only other example in private hands was struck from a different set of dies. 
That example (from the Seck and Hede collections) also has traces of mounting. 
This numismatically and historically highly important and weighty gold type was struck on the orders of the dowager queen 
Sophia on behalf of her son, who was only 14 and had not yet been crowned. The design is a relatively close copy of the 
original Portuguese gold coins that gave their name to this popular trade coin. Rather unconventionally, the central field of 
the coat of arms is occupied by the horseman of Ditmarschen (Ditmarsken) instead of the two bars of Oldenburg and the 
cross of Delmenhorst. Following a complaint by the Council of State this was however changed for the subsequent issues.
€150,000-€200,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Peter v. Hemmert Collection (Copenhagen - 1807) Lot # 11.

========== PAGE 65 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 39
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
13077
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1596. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-55. Dav-8232; Hede-45; Sieg-95; Schou-4; 
Bruun-4624. Weight: 29.59 gms. Mintmaster: Geert van Campen. Engraver: Nicolaus Schwabe. A vibrant luster pen-
etrates the warm toning on this truly grand example of a highly elusive one-year type. 
This is the largest from a set of 6 denominations with a very uniform design for the king’s coronation on 29 August 1596. As 
opposed to the minor coins (16, 8, 4, 2, 1 Skilling) which were used as largesse money, the Specietaler was probably struck 
for presentation pieces for wedding guests and other dignitaries. 
€8,000-€10,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
13078
DENMARK. 2 Skilling, 1596. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-65. Hede-72; Sieg-29; Schou-12; Bru -
un-4628. Mintmaster: Gert van Campen. An absolutely astounding and surely unparalleled example with unbelievable 
luster and lovely deep toning. 
Rare and historically important one-year type struck as largesse money for the coronation on 29 August 1596. 
€1,500-€2,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Peter Hauberg Collection (Copenhagen - 1885) # 370.

13080
DENMARK. 1/4 Speciedaler, 1602. Christian IV . NGC EF Details—Cleaned. KM-48; Hede-48; Sieg-91; Schou-1; 
Bruun-4634. Weight: 7.07 gms. A sharply struck and nicely re-toned example of this very unusual type, believed to have 
been struck in celebration of the marriage between the king’s younger sister, Hedvig and the Duke-Elector of Saxony, 
Christian II on September 12th 1602. 
The present example seems to be the only example outside the National Museum of Denmark, which also owns a unique 
example in gold with the weight of 6 Ducats. Of particular heraldic interest is the fact that the cross of Delmenhorst has been 
given center position on the armoriated reverse. 
€7,000-€10,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Amusing Elephant Type with Pleasing Toning
13081
DENMARK. 8 Solidi, 1603. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-61. KM-15; Hede-74; Sieg-52; Schou-9; 
Aagaard-3 (3/3); Bruun-4653. Weight: 2.95 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Scwabe. Engraver: Nicolaus Schwabe. A superla-
tive, nicely struck example with a shimmering luster and a lovely iridescent toning.
This beautiful and amusing type was according to tradition struck as largesse money to be used in connection with the cel-
ebration of the Hanseatic city of Hamburg swearing allegiance to the Danish king on 30 October 1603.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Page 42 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Otherwise Unobtainable and Beautiful Gold Klippe
13082
DENMARK. 8 Daler Klippe, 1604. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-55. Fr-44; KM-27; Hede-10; Sieg-
148; Schou-1; Bruun-4666. Weight: 17.62 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Superbly struck and retaining copious 
amounts of mint luster. Whereas the 6 and especially the 4 Dalers, albeit rare, sometimes appear on the market, the hefty 
8 Daler is normally considered completely unobtainable. 
Schwabe who had immigrated from Saxony was assigned to the Royal court in 1595 and became the first to produce struck 
medals in Denmark. In 1602 he was appointed mintmaster in Copenhagen where this wonderful gold klippe was struck in 
a number of 409 pieces.
€60,000-€100,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

These “Hungarian” Guldens continued to be struck as “Ducats” until 1802 with the same weight and fineness.

========== PAGE 70 ==========

Page 44 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Historically Important and Unique in Private Hands
13086
DENMARK. Lion Taler, 1608. Elsinore (Helsingør) Mint. Christian IV . NGC VG Details—Cleaned. Dav-3515 (Nor-
way); KM-4 (Norway); Hede-65; Sieg-89; Schou-9; Bruun-4776. Weight: 25.61 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Flemming. Of 
the utmost rarity with the only other registered example being in the National Museum of Denmark. 
The imagery has been quite closely copied from the Dutch so-called Leeuwendaalder (Lion Daalder) and the whole mintage 
of 765 specimens was intended for promoting international trade with the Dutch. The remarkable degree of wear for a coin 
of this size shows that it must have been a success. The axe-bearing Norwegian Lion used on the reverse has wrongly made 
some catalogers believe that the coin itself is also Norwegian. A utterly charming survivor of one of the most interesting types 
from the reign of Christian IV .
€10,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 505 in 
his collection.
Ex: C. F. Pogge (Frankfurt - 1903) Lot # 379.

The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Choice Mint State, Charming, and Immensely Rare
13089
DENMARK. Rose Noble, 1612. Copenhagen Mint; privy mark: Clover. Christian IV . NGC MS-63. Fr-48; KM-51; 
Hede-23A; Sieg-143.1; Schou-3; Bruun-4866. Weight: 8.87 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. The dazzling luster on 
this coin has to be seen to be believed and every detail is as sharp as the day it was minted - a true “piece de resistance” .
The inspiration for this beautiful, amusing and very rare type came from the British Rose Nobles that were widely used in 
international trade. In the contemporary official accounts the type is referred to as a “Guldridder” from the Dutch, “gouden 
rijder”.
€90,000-€130,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

========== PAGE 73 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 47
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
13090
DENMARK. 2 Krone (Corona Danica), 1618. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-53. Dav-3516 ; KM- ; Hede-
105A ; Sieg-87.1; Schou-18; Bruun-5011. Weight: 37.50 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. A light split in the flan 
doesn’t really detract from this example being a most desirable piece with a superb strike and a refined toning.
This beautiful broad Krone (Corona Danica) is in many ways the archetypical coin of Christian IV . The denomination, in-
spired by the English Crown, was introduced in 1618 with the intention that it should pass for 1-½ Speciedaler but the king 
had arranged for the silver to be debased to pocket a sum for his own slender purse. This was of course quickly found out and 
the foreign merchants refused to accept these new “Kroner” at face value.
Among the public, the king’s abbreviated royal motto, RFP (Regnat Firmat Pietas / “Justice strengthens the realms”) was 
soon interpreted as R(iget) F(attes) P(enge) (“The kingdom is in want of money”). 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Frederik Chr. Bech Collection (Copenhagen - 1906) Lot # 186.
    
13091
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1619. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-55. Dav-3514; KM-44; Hede-53C; Sieg-
102,3; Schou-9; Bruun-5072. Weight: 28.69 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Engraver: Nicolaus Schwabe (?). A 
pulchritudinous example showing phenomenal detail, ravishing toning and impressive luster. 
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: H. H. J. Lynge Collection (Copenhagen - 1899) Lot # 599.
Ex: Alfred Benzon Collection (Copenhagen - 1887) Lot # 397.

========== PAGE 74 ==========

Page 48 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
    
13092
DENMARK. 2 Krone, 1619. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . 
NGC AU Details—Cleaned. KM-61; Dav-3516; Hede-105A; 
Sieg-87.4; Schou-20; Bruun-5078. Weight: 37.56 gms. Mintmas-
ter: Nicolaus Schwabe. Hints of luster on the obverse and quite a 
bit more on the reverse, a piece with plenty of eye-appeal.
Imposing in size and design, the ‘Corona Danica’ took inspiration 
to its name from the king’s brother-in-law, James I of England’s sil-
ver Crowns.

A type normally never encountered in nice condition, making 
the present, admirably toned Mint State example a true rarity. 
€1,500-€2,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
JØRGEN VILHELM BERGSØE  
(1835-1911)
Jørgen Vilhelm Bergsøe (1835-1911) was educated as an 
entomologist. Following his relocation to Rome, where 
he became acquainted with Henrik Ibsen, he became a 
successful novelist and poet. In numismatic circles he 
is, however, most famous for having written a work on 
Danish medals which is still used as a reference today.


========== PAGE 75 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 49
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Boldly Struck and Excessively Rare
13094
DENMARK. Gold Krone, 1619. Copenhagen Mint; privy mark: Crossed Clubs. Christian IV . NGC AU-50. Fr-55; 
KM-73; Hede-26B; Sieg-133.2; Schou-3; Bruun-5110. Weight: 2.68 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Post. An exceedingly rare 
type with only a handful of examples known, of which 3 are believed to be in private collections. Slightly wavy flan but 
exceptionally well-struck and with a very nice luster.
When the new silver Krone was introduced in 1618 it was at the same time decided that an issue with the same value (1-1/2 
Speciedaler) should also be minted in gold. When the value of the silver Kroner were reduced to 1-1/3 Speciedaler, the gold 
issues kept their value. All the different denominations minted to this new monetary standard had a crown (krone) as their 
main design.
€20,000-€25,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 76 ==========

Page 50 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
    
13095
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1620. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. Dav-3514; KM-44; 
Hede-53C; Sieg-102.3; Schou-2; Bruun-5153. Weight: 28.79 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Engraver: Nicolaus 
Schwabe (?). Although close inspection reveals light evidence of a past cleaning, this is a superb, overstruck example 
with perfect details, light, warm toning and spectacular luster. 
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Fabulous 2 Gold Krone with a Bird Mintmark
13096
DENMARK. 2 Gold Krone, 1621. Copenhagen Mint; privy mark: bird. Christian IV . NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. 
Fr-54; KM-74.1; Hede-25C; Sieg-134.3; Schou-2; Bruun-5273. Weight: 6.00 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Post. Although ever 
so lightly cleaned, this impressive rarity that is never seen in the market retains most of its original luster. 
The charming bird mintmark was only used by Johan Post from 1620 to 1623.
€15,000-€20,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Frederik Chr. Bech Collection (Copenhagen - 1906) Lot # 228.
Ex: L. Christian Nielsen Collection (Copenhagen - 1878) Lot # 18.

========== PAGE 77 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 51
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Charming and Magnificent Double Taler of the Highest Rarity
13097
DENMARK. 2 Speciedaler, 1623. Gluckstadt Mint. Christian IV . NGC EF-40. Dav-3667; KM-16; Hede-157; Sieg-
184; Schou-7; cf. Lange-32; Bruun-5327. Weight: 58.08 gms. Mintmaster: Albert Dionis. A most attractive example with 
an unusually even strike and lovely toning. This beautiful Double Taler is excessively rare and the present example is 
markedly better than the last example appearing on the market, the Zinck / Knudsen example.
The city of Glückstadt (literally: City of luck) had been founded by Christian IV only seven years earlier, in 1616. The idea 
was to creative a Danish rival to the important City of Hamburg. Promising settlers tax exemption and, radically for the 
time, freedom of religion, the city soon flourished, becoming the home of the first Jewish settlers in Denmark. Many of the 
coins struck at Glückstadt depict Lady Luck standing on a globe, as does this astounding piece.
€25,000-€35,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

Page 52 Stack’s Bowers Galleries

struck, with only limited signs of handling and much retained charm.

========== PAGE 79 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 53
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Spectacular, Hefty Issue of Which Only Two are Known
13101
DENMARK. 3 Speciedaler, 1624-NS. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. Dav-A3525; 
KM-76; Hede-62; Sieg-106; Schou-5; Bruun-5366. Weight: 87.58 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. The only other 
registered example of this beautiful type with “curved” shields is in the National Museum of Denmark. Albeit very 
lightly cleaned, an absolutely spectacular example of this hefty coin.
In an entry of March 31st 1624 the king wrote in one of his diaries that he had received 140 Speciedalers from Schwabe which 
he had struck from Norwegian silver. Even though there might have been silver from other sources in the alloy there can be 
little doubt that the beautiful 1624-series is the first of the Danish / Norwegian coins to be struck from silver actually mined 
at Kongsberg Silver Works which was founded shortly after finding silver ores in the cliffs in the summer of 1623.
€25,000-€35,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Georg F. Timm Collection (Copenhagen - 1831) Lot # 658.

Ex: Frederik Chr. Bech Collection (Copenhagen - 1906) Lot # 
277.
GEORG FRIEDERICH TIMM  
(1746-1829)  
Georg Friederich Timm (1746-1829) worked as a met-
alworker at the royal court and had a highly interesting 
collection of coins and medals, including many rarities. 
The catalog from his estate auction in 1831 was written 
by two of the most prominent numismatists of the time, 
Ole Devegge and Christian Jürgensen Thomsen, and is 
still of considerable scientific value.


========== PAGE 81 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 55
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Near-Gem, Delightful and One of Only Three Known
13105
DENMARK. Rose Noble, 1627. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-64. Fr-48; KM-51; Hede-23A; Sieg-143.1; 
Schou-1; Bruun-5504. Weight: 8.91 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Only three examples of this magnificent coin 
has been registered, the present, the one in the National Museum of Denmark and the one in the collection of Bille-
Brahe that was bought by Bruun and sold at the auction of his duplicates in 1925 (Lot #2014).
An astonishing near-Gem this is the ideal target for even the most discerning of collectors.
The inspiration for this beautiful and amusing type came from the English Rose Nobles that were widely used in interna-
tional trade. In contemporary official accounts the type is referred to as a “Guldridder” from the Dutch, “gouden rijder”. 
The portrait on the 1627 issue is very different from and vastly superior to the ones minted in 1611 - 1613 and would merit 
a type of its own.
€120,000-€150,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 816 in 
his collection.

13106
DENMARK. 2 Speciedaler, 1627-BZ/FA. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-3523; 
KM-104; Hede-56B; Sieg-108.2; Schou-9; Bruun-5508. Weight: 57.38 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe: Engravers: 
Obverse: Balthazar Zwirner, Reverse: (FA). A spectacular example with loads of original luster preserved. 
This one-year portrait type is attributed to the Silesian mintmaster, Balthazar Zwirner, who is known to have been active in 
Oppeln, Breslau and Olmütz c. 1620-1624.

13108
DENMARK. Speciedaler (Reichstaler), 1627. Wolfenbüt -
tel Mint (Lower Saxony). Christian IV . NGC EF Details—
Cleaned. Dav-7758D; cf. KM-66; Hede-3A; Sieg-193.2; Schou-6; 
Aagaard-10; Joseph-240; Bruun-5530. Weight: 28.65 gms. 
Struck for count Philipp Reinhard von Solms-Hohensolms (1613-
1635) as viceregent of the Danish King. Wolfenbüttel in Lower Sax-
ony was controlled by King Christian IV for a brief period during 
the Thirty-Years-War. The coinage produced there in his name was 
made from the gold and silver dinnerware found in the castle.

Boldly Struck and Extremely Rare One-Y ear Type
13111
DENMARK. 2 Gold Krone, 1628-RD. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Fr-Unlisted; 
KM-111; Hede-28; Sieg-135; Schou-2; Bruun-5540. Weight: 5.88 gms. Privy Mark: RD. Extremely rare one-year type.
Struck by “Borgerskabets Mønt” (The Citizens’ Mint) consisting of the mintmaster Matthias Clausen, Johan Brahm and the 
mayor of Copenhagen, Jacob Michelsen. After less than half a year, their right to produce coins was withdrawn. Although 
this type is today so rare that it is totally overlooked in Friedberg, it exists with two highly dissimilar portraits (cf. L. E. Bruun 
Collection, Part I, lot 1036). A bold strike with excellent details.
€20,000-€25,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

ter around the devices on this charming and popular type. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Boldly Struck and Lustrous Trade Ducat
One of Three Known Examples
13113
DENMARK. Ducat, 1640. Gluckstadt Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-55. Fr-37; KM-29; Hede-153A; Sieg-189.1; 
Schou-1; Lange - ; Bruun-5631. Weight: 3.45 gms. Mintmaster: Simon Timpfe, the younger. A lustrous and sharply 
struck example of this excessively rare coin which seems to be known by only three examples, the present, one in The 
National Museum and one other in private hands. 
The present type is a relatively close copy of the Dutch Ducats that enjoyed immense popularity in international trade 
from their introduction at the end of the 16th century to the beginning of the 19th century. The legend in the reverse 
square gives both the denomination and the issuer: DUCATIS REGIÆ MAISTATIS DANI (“The Danish King’s Ducat”). 
Dividing the date is a diminutive rendering of “Lady Luck” .
€15,000-€25,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 61
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Forceful and Very Rare Hebrew Type Double Ducat
13115
DENMARK. 2 Ducat, 1645. Copenhagen Mint; privy mark: Poker. Christian IV . NGC AU-58. Fr-38; KM-140; Hede-
32; Sieg-131.2; Schou-1; Aagaard-TKG2; Bruun-5730. Weight: 6.93 gms. Mintmaster: Heinrich Köhler. Lustrous and 
nicely struck with a bold presentation of the king clad in armor. 
In the middle of the Torstenson War and possibly to invoke the help of God against the Swedes, king Christian personally 
sketched the coin design using the Tetragrammaton (the Hebrew four-letter-name of God) as a key element on silver and 
gold coins struck in Copenhagen, Christiania, and Glückstadt. The 2 Ducat, which was the largest denomination of a total 
of 7 (four in gold and three in silver), is by far the rarest and is only seldom seen in the market.
€12,000-€16,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Peter Frederik Suhm Collection (Copenhagen - 1800-1802) Lot # 55.

AH-35.10.2; Bruun-5749. Mintmaster: Heinrich Köhler. The smallest denomination of the immensely popular “He -
braic” type this coin is, despite its size, simply breathtaking, and unmatched in condition. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Johan G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale -1918), later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 996 
in his collection.

Superlative Mint State and the Only Example Known
13119
DENMARK. Ducat, 1646/5. Glückstadt Mint; privy mark: crossed pokers. Christian IV . NGC MS-61. Fr-unlisted; 
KM-36; Hede-154; Sieg-190.2; Schou-4; Aagaard-TGG2; Bruun-5798. Weight: 3.48 gms. Mintmaster: Jacob Schwieger. 
While the issues minted in Glückstadt bearing Hebrew legends during the reign of Christian IV are all rare, the 1646 
Ducat is however a legendary rarity with the present example being the only one to be registered in both private and 
public collections. The uniqueness doesn’t stop here, as the condition is also decidedly superior to what is normally 
encountered in these gold coins. The flan is extraordinarily even, all details are sharply struck, and bright luster beams 
back at the viewer from all angles.
€80,000-€100,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Handsomely Toned, Highly Detailed and Extremely Rare
13121

Schou-41; Lange-44; Bruun-5802. Weight: 14.16 gms. Mintmaster: Jacob Schwieger. The present type were only struck 
in 1645 and again in 1646 with just the date being altered. Both dates are exceedingly rare and the present beautifully 
struck and nicely toned example easily ranks among the best known.

Stately, Lustrous and Very Rare Hebrew Double Ducat

13123
DENMARK. 3 Speciedaler, ND (1607-1614). Copenhagen Mint (?). Christian IV . NGC EF Details—mount re -
moved, damaged. Dav-LS528; KM-77; Hede-63C; Sieg-114; Schou-17; Bruun-5845. Weight: 86.40 gms. Mintmaster: 
Hans Flemming. A very rare denomination with only two or three in private hands. This stunning presentation coin, 
inspired by the German Lösers, rather unusually shows the king wearing a feathered hat.
The mint at Elsinore (Helsingør), the city of Shakespeare’s Hamlet, was only active from 1607 to 1611, in which period it was 
run by the Dutchman, Hans Flemming.

========== PAGE 92 ==========

Page 66 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Awesome Broad Taler and the Only One in Private Hands
13124
DENMARK. 1 1/2 Speciedaler, ND (1611). Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-LS540; 
KM-154; Hede-64C; Sieg-118; Schou-28; Bruun-5850. Weight: 48.80 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Engraver: 
Isaac Henniges. A very pleasing example of this unusual denomination that has only been registered in two examples, 
the present and the one in the National collection of Denmark.
On this majestic presentation piece the king is clad in armor and carries a marshal’s baton as a symbol of his role as the 
supreme commander in the successful Kalmar War against Sweden. These so-called “breddalere” (“Broad Talers”) became 
so popular that early casts were made to supply the demand, to be used in the lid of tankards, for instance.
€12,000-€18,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.


========== PAGE 93 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 67
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Scarce Clerical Issue
13125
DENMARK. Reichstaler / Speciedaler, 1641-PT. Bremervörde Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Obverse 
Cleaned. Dav-5078; KM-38; Hede-1; Sieg-167; Schou-2; Aagaard-2.1; Lange-61; Bruun-5942. Weight: 29.10 gms. Mint-
master: Peter Timpfe. A very handsome example of this scarce issue. 
In 1634 Frederik became archbishop of Bremen and the following year bishop of Verden. After the Torstenson Feud (part of 
the European Thirty Years’ War) and the Treaty of Brömsebro in 1645 both territories were, however, sadly lost to Sweden.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: H. H. J. Lynge Collection (Copenhagen - 1899) Lot # 1011.

========== PAGE 94 ==========

Page 68 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Wonderful and Very Rare Largesse Double Ducat
13126
DENMARK. 2 Ducat Klippe, 1648. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-58. Fr-74; KM-164; Hede-1; Sieg-127; 
Schou-26; Aagaard-4.2; Bruun-5981. Weight: 6.75 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. Engraver: Hans zum Busch. A most 
enchanting example of the rare and popular Double Ducat.
For the celebration of the king’s coronation on the 23rd of November a series of unusual coins were minted on square flans to 
be used as largesse money. Silver coins with a total value of 1000 Rigsdalers were minted in denominations of 1/4, 1/6 and 
1/12 Speciedaler to be thrown to the crowd in the streets of Copenhagen. It has been speculated that the reason for making 
the coin square was so they would not roll down the open sewers. Gold coins were also minted as Ducats and 1/2 Ducats 
that might have been used in the same way, whereas the 5, 4, 3 and 2 Ducats were minted for the personal use of the king 
and the chancellor, Corfitz Ulfeldt. The mintage of the Double Ducat is not known, but although very rare, the mintage of 
only 5 given by H. Hede is clearly incorrect.
The reverse has been interpreted as either an urn spewing ashes or as an incense burner. On the receptacle is written, TAN-
DEM, the Latin word for “finally” which might refer to the fact that Christian IV , after nearly 60 years on the throne, had 
finally gone to meet his maker.
€8,000-€10,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 95 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 69
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
13127
DENMARK. 1/4 Speciedaler, Klippe, 1648. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. KM-161; Hede-47; Sieg-
67; Schou-32; Bruun-5986. Weight: 7.14 gms. Mintmaster: Heinrich Köhler. Largesse Klippe struck on the coronation 
1648 and the largest of the silver denominations of these curious and fascinating Klippes. Fully worthy of its high grade 
and gorgeously toned. 
€1,800-€2,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ravishing Roman Portrait in Superlative Mint State Condition
13128
DENMARK. Ducat, 1650. Copenhagen Mint; privy mark: Poker. Frederik III. NGC MS-63. Fr-77; KM-171; Hede-
7; Sieg-109; Schou-1; Aagaard-8.2; Bruun-6015. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. Engraver: Hans zum 
Busch. An eminently dazzling example of this excessively rare date of which only 2 or 3 are in private hands. The obverse 
shows Frederik III dressed as a Roman emperor wearing toga and a laurel wreath, while the king’s royal motto: DOM-
INVS PROVIDEBIT (“The Lord will provide”) takes an uncommonly prominent place on the reverse. 
It clearly emerges from mintmaster Heinrich Köhler’s accounts that this early Ducat wasn’t minted at the request of the king 
but for the use of wealthy private citizens.
€12,000-€15,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

Handsome Mint State Gold Krone 
The Best of Only Two in Private Hands
13133
DENMARK. Gold Krone, 1655. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-60. Fr-120; KM-206-1; Hede-43A; Sieg-
142.1; Schou-3; Aagaard-18.1; Bruun-6175. Weight: 5.97 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. Engraver: Matthias Först. 
Wonderfully lustrous and very sharply struck. To the best of our knowledge there is only one additional example in 
private hands, namely the one from the Guildal collection which was sold at the L. E. Bruun collection of duplicates in 
1925 (Lot #2434).
Although the present type was only minted in Copenhagen in 1655 it was continued in Glückstadt from 1657 to 1660 with 
dies provided from Copenhagen.
€18,000-€22,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

Charming Ship Quadruple Ducat  
Less than a Handful of Examples Registered
13137
DENMARK. 4 Ducats, 1657. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-58. Fr-110; KM-217.1; Hede-30A; Sieg-135.1; 
Schou-2; Aagaard-19.1; Bruun-6218. Weight: 13.75 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. Engraver: Matthias Först. With 
luster beaming from the rims and with impressive details, this is truly a superlative example of a majestic Quadruple 
Ducat of which less than a handful of examples are known. 
The beautifully rendered full-rigged ship on the reverse is used to symbolize the fact that the coin itself had been struck with 
gold brought back from overseas.
€40,000-€60,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

========== PAGE 104 ==========

Page 78 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Impressive and Striking Half Portugaloser
13140
DENMARK. 5 Ducats, 1659. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. Fr-unlisted (cf. 124); 
KM-PnJ16; Hede-100A; Sieg-53.1; Schou-5; Aagaard-76.1 (59-E3/59-E1); Bruun-6275. Weight: 17.16 gms. Mintmaster: 
Henrik Köhler. Although at some point gently wiped this imposing piece still retains considerable original luster, and 
the many details are magnificently preserved. All 5 Ducats of the “Eben Ezer” type are rare but the present variant giving 
the denomination of “IIII Marck” is excessively so.
This iconic type was struck to commemorate the failure of the Swedish king, Carl X Gustav, to capture Copenhagen. The 
monogram of King Frederik III is shown above a stone with the text EBEN EZER, referring to the Old Testament (1 Samuel 
7:12) in which Samuel erected a “stone of help” (Eben-ha-Ezer) between Mizpah and Jeshanah to honor the Lord for his help 
in securing the Israelites a victory over the Philistines.
The Latin inscription on the reverse, SOLI DEO GLORIA, is another tribute to God (“To God alone be the glory”) while 
the dramatic design shows the hand of God (Manus Dei) severing the hand of the Swedish king with the sword of justice as 
he reaches out for the Danish crown. It has been suggested that the very rare pieces struck in gold might have been used as 
distinctions given to officers in the civic guard who had distinguished themselves during the siege.
€90,000-€110,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 105 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 79
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT

Aagaard-73.1; Schou-30; Bruun-6278. Weight: 44.54 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. An absolutely stunning example 
retaining excellent luster. 
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: K. M. Lind Collection (Copenhagen - 1907) Lot # 436.

Johan Blum (reverse). A rare type in an astonishing condition retaining most of the original luster from the day the coin

Page 80 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Wonderful, Exceedingly Rare and Interesting Ducat
13144
DENMARK. Ducat, 1660-IS. Glückstadt Mint. Frederik III. NGC Unc Details—Scratches. Fr-cf. 79; KM-50; Hede-
142; Sieg-157; Schou-5; Aagaard-5.1; Lange-45; Bruun-6345. Weight: 3.45 gms. Mintmaster: Jacob Schwieger. A won-
derful example of this exceptionally rare one-year type combining the design of the early Copenhagen Ducats with the 
king in Roman attire with a charming rendering of an unusually petite Lady Luck on her globe. Only five examples have 
been registered: one in the National Museum of Copenhagen (mounted); one in Vienna, one in Schloss Gotha (missing), 
and a further example in private hands (has been mounted).
€12,000-€18,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
13145
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1661-HK. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-53. Dav-3546A; KM-238; Hede-57B; 
Sieg-75.2; Aagaard-91.2; Schou-14; Bruun-6370. Weight: 28.05 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. Engraver: Johan Blum. 
A highly detailed and attractively toned example of a scarce Taler type. 
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 107 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 81
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Stunning Portugaloser Struck for the King
One of Only Two Known
13146
DENMARK. Portugaloser (10 Ducats), 1662-IS. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Reverse Graf -
fiti. Fr-113; KM-unlisted; Hede-61; Sieg-79; Schou-1; Aagaard-97.1; Bruun-6382. Weight: 34.67 gms. Mintmaster: Jo-
han Stichmann. Engraver: Johan Blum. With only minute imperfections as a small dent in the obverse right field and 
the interesting owner’s mark (?) in the background field of the Swedish crowns, this is a wonderfully impressive example 
of a supremely desirable regal issue. Apart from two examples in the National Museum of Denmark (one in gold, one 
in silver), this is the only registered example of this issue dated 1662. The precise weight of the two existing gold pieces 
(34.67, 34.71 gms) clearly indicates that they were struck as 10 Ducats, obviously in a very small number and probably 
for the personal use of the king.
The engraver Johan Blum (ca. 1599 - 1669) had been working as an engraver at the Mint in Bremen. In 1657 he was ap-
pointed medalist by Frederik III and married a Dane. He was technically very accomplished and artistically his work is 
inspired by Sebastian Dadler.
€135,000-€165,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

========== PAGE 108 ==========

Page 82 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Nearly-Choice with Excellent Details
13147
DENMARK. Ducat, 1662. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. Fr-101; KM-236; Hede-16B; Sieg-116.2; 
Schou-11; Aagaard-31.1; Bruun-6388. Weight: 3.48 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Stichmann. Engraver: Henrik Köhler (ob-
verse), Johan Blum (reverse). Very rare in this exceptional condition with both details and luster being way above what 
is normally encountered.
Re-using an old Köhler-die for the obverse, Stichmann had these Ducats struck in a large town-house in Købmagergade 
in the center of Copenhagen. The house later became the home of the famous Danish-Norwegian author, Ludvig Holberg.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 1445a in his collec-
tion.

13148
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1663-IS. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-60. Fr-117; KM-244; Hede-33B; Sieg-
131.2; Schou-7; Aagaard-36.2; Bruun-6401. Weight: gms. Mintmaster: Johan Stichmann. Engraver: Johan Blum. Ex -
tremely rare. Highly brilliant and attractive example.
This beautiful gold type clearly mimics Blum’s Taler-type from 1662. The mintmaster and goldsmith Johan Stichmann is 
known to have been in charge of a part of the militia when Copenhagen was unsuccessfully stormed by the Swedes on 11 
February 1659.

Handsome and Unusually Designed Ducat

13149
DENMARK. Ducat, 1664. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-55. Fr-104; KM-252; Hede-23; Sieg-122; 
Schou-9; Aagaard-41.1; Bruun-6417. Weight: 3.47 gms. Mintmaster: Caspar Herbach. Engraver: Johan Blum. A beauti-
fully preserved example of this unusual type of which the only other registered example resides at the National Museum 
of Denmark.
This is the very first time since the medieval period that a Danish monarch is portrayed rendering the head only.

Magnificent Half Portugaloser Struck for the King

13153
DENMARK. 5 Ducats, 1665-FCH. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-61. Fr-Unlisted; KM-Unlisted; Hede-
68A; Sieg-86.1; Schou-3; Aagaard-106.1 (Aagaard erroneously gives a reference to Bruun-6436); Bruun-6435. Weight: 
17.30 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Caspar Herbach. Engraver: Jeremias Hercules. A stunningly vibrant and beautiful 
example of this commanding gold piece which was most likely struck for the king’s personal use. Presumably unique in 
private hands with the only other registered example in the National Museum of Denmark. 
The mintmaster Frederik Caspar Herbach took over the position from his father, Caspar Herbach in 1664 but was fired for 
fraud by Christian V in 1670.
€150,000-€200,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 87

Grandiose 5 Ducats Known by Only Two Examples
13154
DENMARK. 5 Ducats (1/2 Portugaloser), 1665-GK. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC Unc Details—Edge Re -
paired. Fr-84; KM-unlisted; Hede-107A ; Sieg-36.2; Schou-5; Aagaard-148.1 (65-2/65.1); Bruun-6437. Weight: 17.22 
gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger. This spectacular piece which is only known in two examples (this and the one in the 
Royal Coin Cabinet in Copenhagen), could be a pattern for a silver 2 Mark struck in gold to be used as a presentation 
piece for the king. No examples in silver are however known using this particular set of dies.
€80,000-€100,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 114 ==========

Page 88 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Highly Appealing and of the Utmost Rarity
13155
DENMARK. Ducat, 1665. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-58. Fr-unlisted (cf. 101); KM-265.2; Hede-24B; 
Sieg-123.2; Schou-7; Aagaard-48.1; Bruun-6438. Weight: 3.44 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Caspar Herbach. Engraver: 
Jeremias Hercules. A beautifully preserved example with a strong and exquisitely rendered portrait of the king.
Frederik Caspar Herbach continued the design used by his two predecessors but asked Hercules to make the dies a little 
smaller. The result was very positive but only very few specimens were minted, and today this type, which was minted in 
both 1665 and 1666, is so rare that from both dates only 4 examples have been registered: the present, one in St. Petersburg 
(1666) and 2 in the Danish National Museum (1665, 1666).
€20,000-€30,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

Engraver: Jeremias Hercules. A superb, light rainbow toning frames Hercules’ grandiose portrait in a most alluring way, 
making this a highly desirable example of an excessively rare Double Taler.

type with the king laureled, draped and armored. Disregarding the 1651 and 1652 Kroner (with their primitive portraits) 
this is the only portrait Krone of Frederik III. The exquisite condition makes it a gem in any collection.

Wonderfully Detailed Lady Luck Type
13159

Sieg-159; Schou-9; Aagaard-7.1; Lange-65; Bruun-6557. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Woltereck. A scarce type 
in a much better condition than normally seen with both the king’s portrait and the rendering of Fortuna being very 
detailed.

13160
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1667-GK. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. Dav-3555A; KM-288; Hede-73B;

Page 94 Stack’s Bowers Galleries

13163
DENMARK. 4 Mark, 1668-GK (Archbishopric of Salzburg countermark). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC 
AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-3581; KM-274; Hede-113A; Sieg-59.1; Aagaard-116.3; Schou-29; Bruun-6655. Weight: 
21.54 gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger. The obverse right field bears a clearly struck oval countermark from the Arch-
bishopric of Salzburg, extremely rare thus. 
In 1681 the Archbishop of Salzburg, Max Gandolph of Kuenberg instructed that all foreign silver coins should be counter-
marked for a charge and the value lowered as many of the circulating coins were deemed base.
(For another example of countermarked coinage from Salzburg, see lot 14047). 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Admirable Double Taler 
The Only One in Private Hands
13166
DENMARK. 2 Speciedaler, 1669/8-FCH. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-3558; 
KM-302; Hede-79A; Sieg-95.1; Aagaard-111.2; Schou-9; Bruun-6694. Weight: 57.71 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Caspar 
Herbach. Engraver: Jeremias Hercules. Superbly detailed and exquisitely re-toned after an old, gentle cleaning. This ma-

date, which is tucked away at the horizontal arms of the cross, has been altered from 1668 to 1669.

Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
13167
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1669. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-55. Dav-3561; KM-307; Hede-80B; Sieg-
96.2; Schou-14; Aagaard-119.1; Bruun-6698. Weight: 29.19 gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger. A splendid example of 
this excessively rare lettered edge type with less than a handful in private hands.
Frederik III died on February 9th 1670 and this was the last Speciedaler to be struck for him. in fact, the obverse die was 
reused for the Speciedaler made for the succession (KM-321; Hede-60).

========== PAGE 124 ==========

Page 98 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Beautiful, Signed Double Ducat 
The Only One in Private Hands
13169
NORW AY . 2 Ducats, ND (1669-70?)-JL. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-58. Fr-5b; KM-A119 (mistakenly 
listed with date 1670); Hede-39 (Denmark); Sieg-73 (139); NMD-5A; Schou-4; Aagaard-7.1; Bruun-6737. Weight: 6.81 
gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. Engraver: Jonas Olufsen Lunder. 
A breathtakingly noble example of this very serene and unusual issue that easily ranks among the most beautiful Norwe-
gian coins ever minted. Of extraordinary rarity and numismatic importance as this seems to be the only privately owned 
example bearing Lunder’s initials.
As this type doesn’t bear St. Olavs’ axe-bearing lion or for that matter other design elements normally associated with Nor-
way it has until recently been assumed to have been struck in Copenhagen. The initialed signature on the reverse (IL) of this 
particular variant however clearly points to Jonas Lunder who worked as an engraver at the Mint in Christiania from c. 
1664 - 1682. Jonas Lunder was the first of a Norwegian “dynasty” of engravers comprising Anders Jonsen Lunder, the elder, 
Gabriel Andersen Lunder, and Anders Jonsen Lunder, the younger.
This example was formerly in the collection of merchant and shipowner Peter von Hemmert / van Hemert (1734-1810), who 
functioned as one of the directors in the Danish Asiatic Company from 1776 to 1783. Partly due to the general economic 
downturn for merchant ships following the end of the American Revolutionary War and partly due to an embezzlement 
scandal at the Asiatic Company, von Hemmert was financially brought to his knees. The downfall culminated in 1805 when 
his company was taken into bankruptcy, and only two years later, he was forced to auction off his coin collection, which was 
astoundingly rich in large and excessively rare gold coins.
€35,000-€45,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Peter v. Hemmert Collection (Copenhagen - 1807) Lot # 114.

========== PAGE 125 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 99
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
13170
DENMARK. Silver Off-Metal Strike 10 Ducats, ND (1669)-GK. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—
Reverse Damage. Fr-cf. 94; KM-unlisted (cf. 314, gold); Hede-36; Sieg-141; Schou-19a; Aagaard-63.1; Bruun-6747. 
Weight: 11.37 gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger. Scarce and with a splendid portrait. As the weight of the known silver 
off-strikes vary considerably in weight they were probably struck as presentations pieces rather than as coins with a fixed 
value. 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Frederik Chr. Bech Collection (Copenhagen - 1906) Lot # 641.
Ex: Ole Devegge Collection (Copenhagen - 1851) Lot # 2859.
PETER VON HEMMERT / V AN HEMERT (1734-1810) 
Peter von Hemmert / van Hemert (1734-1810), was a merchant and 
shipowner who functioned as one of the directors in the Danish 
Asiatic Company from 1776 to 1783. Partly due to the general eco -
nomic downturn for merchant ships following the end of the Ameri-
can Revolutionary War and partly due to an embezzlement scandal 
at the Asiatic Company, von Hemmert was financially brought to 
his knees. The downfall culminated in 1805 when his company was 
taken into bankruptcy. Just two years later, he was forced to sell his 
coin collection, which was astoundingly rich in large and excessively 
rare gold coins, at auction.

Enormously Rare and Absolutely Stunning Double Ducat
13171
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1670-GK. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-62. Fr-136; KM-328; Hede-1; Sieg-117; 
Schou-3; Bruun-6753. Weight: 6.97 gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger. Engraver: Jeremias Hercules. A decidedly supe-
rior example with an astonishing luster and crystal clear details. An impossibly rare type struck on Christian V’s ascent 
to the throne. The issue is quite unusual because it was designed only for a Double Ducat to be struck on a broad flan. 
The portrait of the only 24 year-old king is widely considered the best of the early numismatic portraits of the king.
€40,000-€50,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 128 ==========

Page 102 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Engaging Taler Struck for the Danish East India Company
13174
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1671-GK. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. Dav-409; KM-
317; Hede-63A; Sieg-60.1; Schou-7; Bruun-6759. Weight: 28.89 gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger. Engraver: Jeremias 
Hercules. Albeit lightly wiped, a most impressive and rare trade coin struck for the Danish East India Company.
The Danish East India Company (Dansk Ostindisk Compagni) was created on the initiative of Christian IV as the first 
Danish shareholding company. The king was the largest shareholder, but among the investors were nobility and citizens 
in Denmark, Norway and the duchies. The intention with the establishment of the company was to gain a share in the 
trade in spices, silk and cotton on a par with Holland and England, and the company was given a monopoly on trade with 
Asia by sea. In 1618 the first expedition was sent, and as a result of this expedition the trading station of Tranquebar was 
established on the Coromandel coast in south-east India. In the first years the financial yield was, however, modest and 
the company was dissolved in 1650. It was not until 1668 that a ship was again sent from Denmark-Norway to Asia, and 
in 1670 a new East India Company was established. As the trading goods were predominantly paid for in silver, the newly 
started company funded the present mintage, which they had obtained the privilege to mint at the Royal Mint in Copenha-
gen. At the end of the 17th century, trade experienced better business conditions and it was possible to send out an average 
of two ships per year.
€7,000-€9,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 129 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 103
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT

115A; Sieg-142.1; Schou-3; Aagaard-1.1; Bruun-6791. Weight: 3.44 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Woltereck. Mounting trace 
with minute daylight. Scarce.

========== PAGE 131 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 105
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Choice Mint State Ducat with Blazing Luster
13180
DENMARK. Ducat, 1680-CW . Glückstadt Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-63. Fr-141; KM-70.1; Hede-117; Sieg-144.1; 
Schou-4; Aagaard-5.1; Bruun-6913. Weight: 3.44 gms. Mintmaster: Christopher Woltereck. An eminently lustrous ex-
ample with razor-sharp details. The Ducats struck in Glückstadt differ from the ones struck in Copenhagen in being 
minted for regular trade use rather than just as occasion coins.
Across the series of Ducats struck at Glückstadt between 1672 and 1685, the portraits of Christian V are numerous, with 
varying degrees of true likeness. It therefore seems likely that a number of different (unnamed) engravers and goldsmiths 
were employed when required by a given demand for gold.
€9,000-€11,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 132 ==========

Page 106 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Sublime Colonial Ducat with an Unusual Architectural Reverse
13181
DENMARK. Ducat, 1682-GS. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-62. Fr-148; KM-372; Hede-11; Sieg-89; 
Schou-1; Bruun-6957. Weight: 3.47 gms. Mintmaster: Gregorius Sesemann. Engraver: Jeremias Hercules. Noting an ir-
regularity in the flan on the obverse, this is a positively premium example with remarkable luster and astonishing details.
On the reverse of this unusual type is seen the layout of the Danish fortress, Frederiksborg in Guinea. Three years after this 
issue the fort was sold to the British who renamed it Port Royal.
€11,000-€14,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Alfred Benzon Collection (Copenhagen - 1887) Lot # 1146.

========== PAGE 133 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 107
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
The Bille-Brahe family, one of the most important noble families in the his -
tory of Denmark, can be traced to the Middle Ages. The family name arose 
from a merger of the two noble families Bille and Brahe. There have been 
many prominent members of the family over the years, but the most famous 
by far is world-renowned astronomer Tycho Brahe (1546-1601), son of Otto 
Brahe and Beate Clausdatter Bille. 
The most significant collector in the Bille-Brahe family was Count Preben 
Bille-Brahe (1773-1857), who is seen here with his second wife, Johanne Car-
oline Wilhelmine b. Falbe.
They were portrayed in 1817 by C.W . Eckersberg, one of Denmark’s most tal-
ented painters from the time art historians refer to as Denmark’s golden age. It 
shows a tanned man who was out on the farm during the day and in the eve-
ning sat with a book in his hand, together with his spouse, who, of course, was 
occupied with her needlework. The painting can be seen at the Ny Carlsberg 
Glyptotek in Copenhagen.
As a young man, Preben Bille-Brahe trained as an officer in the horse guard, 
after which (as the times dictated), he set off on a Grand Tour, which led him 
to Italy. It was there his great interest in art and history, so exquisitely reflected in his coin collection, was truly awak-
ened. He also visited the British Isles to study agriculture to benefit the agricultural exploitation of the family estate, 
Hvedholm. In 1798, the Countship of Brahesminde was established by uniting the lands from Hvedholm, Damsbo, 
Stensgård and Østrupgård as well as the two islands Avernakø and Drejø. Preben Bille-Brahe was considered both a 
skilled and humane estate administrator, particularly active in improving the living conditions of small-scale work-
ers, village teachers, etc. He not only had humanistic interests, but was also generally interested in science; it is by no 
means accidental that Eckersberg let Bille-Brahe hold a book in his hand. The count’s personal library at Hvedholm 
was eagerly added to, but he also supported the creation of a public library on Funen. In 1840 he received the Grand 
Cross of the Order of Dannebrog and in 1841 he was appointed Privy Councilor to the court. 
His son, diplomat Henrik Bille-Brahe (1798-1875) and grandson, Preben Charles Bille-Brahe Selby (1842-1918) 
continued to increase the collection. But at the disentailing of the estate in 1920, the countship’s collection held in 
Hvedholm Manor had to be disposed of in favor of the Treasury. The collection was valued at DKK 175,000 by Julius 
W . Wilcke and H. H. Schou, who prepared the main 
part of the L. E. Bruun catalog. With the intervention 
of Georg Galster (who was head of the coin cabinet in 
the National Museum, to which L. E. Bruun’s collection 
would be bequeathed in the unlikely event that the col-
lection held here should be destroyed or stolen within 
the next 100 years), it was decided to accept Bruun’s 
bid of DKK 140,000, and the deal was completed on 21 
October, 1922. 
Many of the most sublime pieces in L. E. Bruun’s collec-
tion come from the collection of the Countship of Bra-
hesminde, and there is no doubt that many specimens 
among the 19th century coins went straight from the 
Mint into this collection. The Bille-Brahe family’s close 
relationship with other Danish noble families, where 
exceptional pieces had been treasured for generations, 
enabled them to acquire coins that would otherwise 
not have been available to collectors. 
THE COLLECTION OF THE COUNTSHIP OF BRAHESMINDE
Count Preben Bille-Brahe with his second 
wife, Johanne Caroline Wilhelmine b. Falbe.
Aerial view of Hvedholm Castle.

========== PAGE 134 ==========

The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Choice One-Y ear Type with Stunning Details and Alluring Toning
13182
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1683. Glückstadt Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-63. Dav-3677; KM-73; Hede-120; Sieg-140; 
Schou-3; Lange-82A; Bruun-6987. Weight: 28.95 gms. Mintmaster: Christopher Woltereck. An absolute beauty with 
flashing fields and a lovely peripheral toning. Very rare one-year type and the only Taler type struck for Christian V in 
Glückstadt. Although the original mintage isn’t known it must have been very low.
€18,000-€22,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 135 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 109
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Hefty, Nearly-Choice Quadruple Ducat Featuring Fort Christiansborg
13183
DENMARK. 4 Ducats, 1688. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-62. Fr-166; KM-396; Hede-24; Sieg-95; 
Schou-1; Bruun-7034. Weight: 13.80 gms. Mintmaster: Gregorius Sesemann. Lettered edge: PIETATE ET IUSTITIA 
(royal motto: “Piety and Justice”). Apart from the one in the National Museum of Denmark, this seems to be the only 
existing example. A stunning strike of this exceptionally hefty coin combine with vibrant luster to make this a piece 
worthy of the most discerning collector’s cabinet.
The reverse offers a bird’s eye view of the Danish outpost on Africa’s Gold Coast, Fort Christiansborg (now Osu Castle), 
located in what is now Ghana along the Gulf of Guinea. The fortress became the Danish administrative headquarters of 
Guinea in 1685 and was predominantly used as a place of embarkation of enslaved local captives for the Danish colonies in 
the Caribbean.
€40,000-€60,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Fort Christiansborg

========== PAGE 136 ==========

Page 110 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Gorgeously Toned Choice Mint State Krone 
Only Three Known
13184
DENMARK. Krone, 1691-CW . Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-63. Dav-unlisted; KM-unlisted; Hede-94; 
Sieg-33; Schou-30; Aagaard-61; Bruun-7078. Weight: 22.37 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke, the elder. A ravishing 
example with incredible toning and plenty of multicolored luster from the fields surrounding the lightly matte devices. 
Only 3 examples of this type are known: the present, one in the National Museum of Denmark and one in private hands 
that was sold as lot 3086 at the 1925 Bruun collection of duplicates (later coll. Hede III (1994, lot 415) and Zinck IV 
(2002, lot 179). Bearing the denomination 2 Mark, there can be little doubt that this elegant issue, as Aagaard suggests, 
was made as a presentation piece.
€8,000-€10,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

13185
DENMARK. Ducat, 1692-CW . Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-64. Fr-156; KM-415.1; Hede-31A; Sieg-

This excessively rare type that is almost never seen on the market was struck between the death of Jeremias Hercules and 
the appointment of Anton Meybusch. The die for the amusing and highly unusual portrait was clearly cut by an engraver 
without the best artistic qualifications.

Imposing, Extremely Rare, and Vividly Lustrous Double Krone
13188

Apart from the Karsten Kold example sold in 2022, we haven’t been able to trace another privately held example. That

and thus vastly inferior to the present ethereal piece boasting wonderful luster and lovely toning.

========== PAGE 142 ==========

Page 116 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Near-Gem Double Ducat of the Highest Rarity 
An Intriguing Design
13191
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1699. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-64. Fr-169; KM-457; Hede-54; Sieg-112; 
Schou-1; Kold-147a; Bruun-7232. Weight: 6.91 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Engraver: Anton Meybusch. Con-
dition-wise a marvelous near-Gem of borderline perfection, and on top of that a historically significant piece which as a 
Double Ducat is only known by a handful examples in private ownership.
On the stern of the ship lying in front of the Christiansborg fort in Guinea can be seen the initials, DWC (Dansk Westin-
disk Compagnie / Danish West India Company) of the trading company that had commissioned this issue.
The Danish West India Company was formally chartered by King Christian V in 1671. The company administered the 
Danish West Indies until 1754, when it passed to the Danish Government. It played a vital part in the North Atlantic trian-
gular trade where enslaved Africans from the Gold Coast were traded for molasses and rum in the West Indies. The Danish 
West Indies, which were eventually sold to the United States in 1917 (presently U.S. Virgin Islands), consisted of St. Thomas 
(settled in 1668), St. John (settled in 1671/1718) and St. Croix (bought from the French in 1733).
€40,000-€50,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Vilhelm Bergsøe Collection (Copenhagen - 1890) Lot # 317.

========== PAGE 143 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 117
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
13192
DENMARK. Silver Off-Metal Strike Ducat, ND (1675). Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-63. Fr-cf. 192; 
KM-Pn26; Hede-7; Sieg-86; Schou-6a; Bruun-7238. Weight: 2.54 gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger. Toned and lustrous 
silver off-strike of the fat little Ducat. The same dies were used for the Double Ducat which is why the 1928 Bruun cata-
logue refers to this as such. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Near-Gem Copenhagen City View Ducat 
With Brilliant Luster and Incredible Details
13196
DENMARK. Ducat, ND. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-64. Fr-205; KM-454; Hede-55; Sieg-113; Schou-25; 
Bruun-7256. Weight: 3.48 gms. A stunning near-Gem with baffling details, a completely even flan and an absolutely un-
surpassed luster for the type.
Underneath the text “Hafnia Dania” (Copenhagen - Denmark) is shown a magnificent view of the capital seen from the 
harbor, from which it clearly emerges that it was not without a reason that Copenhagen was dubbed, “City of Towers”. The 
amusing obverse with the personification of the winds blowing from the clouds over a monogrammed pyramid, bears the 
legend: NEC VI NEC METU (“Neither with violence, nor fear”) referring to the just reign of the king.
€7,000-€10,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Otto Diemar Collection (Copenhagen - 1901) Lot # 1038.

========== PAGE 147 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 121
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Choice Mint State Watchman’s Taler
13198
DENMARK. Speciedaler, ND. Glückstadt Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-63. KM-74.2; Dav-3676; Hede-119; Sieg-141; 
Schou-45; Lange-82; Aagaard-Vsp-1; Bruun-7274. Weight: 28.95 gms. Mintmaster: Christopher Woltereck. A razor 
sharp strike, brilliant luster and nice even iridescent toning combine to make this a truly exceptional example with 
amazing eye appeal and worthy of any highly advanced collection.
This highly interesting, very unusual and exceptionally rare one-year type bears the edge lettering: SI VIGILANT ALII ET 
ME VIGILARE DECET (“When others watch, so should I”) for which reason it is commonly referred to as “The Watchman 
Taler”. The type is believed to have been struck for a particular occasion where the utmost vigilance of both king and country 
was called upon.
€15,000-€25,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
13199
DENMARK. Silver Off-Metal Strike 3 Ducats, ND (1699). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-63. KM-Pn37; 
Hede-1; Sieg-52; Schou-1e; Kold-109; Bruun-7289. Weight: 5.91 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Engraver: Anton 
Meybusch (A.MEIBUS.F .). Silver strike of this beautiful accession striking with almost proof-like surfaces. 
While listed as a coin in KM, Friedberg (in gold), Hede, and Sieg, Karsten Kold makes a compelling argument in favor of 
this being a medal or at least a medallic issue. He argues that the name of the engraver was only allowed on medals at the 
time, and on this specimen it is written in full on both sides. Further evidence he finds in that the reverse die was used for a 
medal commiserating the death of Christian V (Bruun-15447). 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 148 ==========

Page 122 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Attractive Triple Ducat with Only One Other Example Registered
13200
DENMARK. 3 Ducats, ND (1699). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-58. Fr-207; KM-463; Hede-4; Sieg-37; 
Schou-3; Kold-148b; Bruun-7290. Weight: 10.33 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Engraver: Anton Meybusch. In 
spite of a minute edge nick, the abundant luster and the excellent details make this completely on a par with the only 
other example registered, the one in the National Museum of Denmark.
This hefty triple Ducat struck in commemoration of the royal succession on August 25th 1699 was struck with the same dies 
as those used for the regular one Ducat.
€40,000-€50,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Peter Pedersen Collection (Private Sale - Before 1861).

========== PAGE 149 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 123
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Awe-inspiring and Lustrous 10 Ducats
The Largest Gold Coin Struck for Frederik IV
One of Only Two in Existence
13201
DENMARK. 10 Ducats, 1699. Copenhagen Mint; mm: heart. Frederik IV . NGC MS-60. Fr-213; KM-Pn39; Hede-36; 
Sieg-17; Schou-6; Kold-149a; Bruun-7296. Weight: 33.93 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Engraver: Anton Mey-
busch. Light edge-nick, but otherwise an awe-inspiring piece with large lustrous fields and lovely details. Exceptional 
off-strike in gold known in only two examples, the present and the one in the National Museum of Denmark and the 
largest of all gold coins struck for Frederik IV . 
The engraver, Anton Meybusch (born ca. 1645 in Essen - died May 2nd 1702 in Copenhagen) was a remarkable engraver 
of both coins and medals, and before coming to Copenhagen in 1691 he had done exquisite work in Sweden, Baden-Würt-
temberg, Hamburg and even for three years for the Sun King, Louis XIV of France. Equally important to his artistic work 
were the massive technical improvements that were implemented at the Royal Mint in Copenhagen under his direction. At 
the center of the reverse is shown a radiant triangle with a stylized Hebrew Tetragrammaton.
€80,000-€100,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Page 124 Stack’s Bowers Galleries

21 (Denmark); Sieg-13 (Norway); NMD-2; Schou-8; Kold-170;

topher Meyer. Engraver: Caspar Barth. A grandiose example of 
this hefty issue struck on the succession of throne with the fol-
lowing edge lettering: ET NORDENS LYYS GIK UD - ET AN -
DET TÆNDTE GUD (“ A Northern light went out - another was 
lit by God”). 
Recent die studies have proved that this issue which were formerly 
ascribed to the Mint in Copenhagen is actually Norwegian.

Near Gem Uncirculated Ducat Struck from African Gold
13208
DENMARK. Ducat, 1708. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-64. Fr-244; KM-484; Hede-11B; Sieg-42.2; 
Schou-5; Bruun-7357. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke, the younger. A near Gem Uncirculated ex -
ample with an unbelievable luster and sublime details.
A very rare type struck from African gold. The reverse shows the Danish outpost on Africa’s Gold Coast, Fort Christiansborg 
(now Osu Castle), located in what is now Ghana along the Gulf of Guinea. The fortress became the Danish administrative 
headquarters of Guinea in 1685 and was predominantly used as a place of embarkation of enslaved local captives for the 
Danish colonies in the Caribbean.
€10,000-€13,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Choice Mint State Ducat from a Very Rare Mint
13211
DENMARK. Ducat, 1718-BH. Rendsburg Mint. Frederik IV . NGC MS-63. Fr-226; KM-8; Hede-60; Sieg-61; Schou-1; 
Lange- ; Bruun-7465. Weight: 3.44 gms. Mintmaster: Bastian Hille. Engraver: Gottfried Metelles. Sharply struck and 
with considerable luster remaining in the protected areas. Whereas the Half Ducat from Rendsburg is encountered from 
time to time, the Ducat is of the utmost rarity. 
The engraver Gottfried Wilhelm Metelles was also active at the Mints at Minden and Königsberg.
€10,000-€12,000

13212

Cleaned. Fr-227; KM-7; Hede-61; Sieg-60; Schou-2; Lange-100; Bruun-7482. Weight: 1.73 gms. Mintmaster: Bastian 
Hille. Engraver: Gottfried Metelles. Superior example of this scarce one-year type minted in the fortified city of Rends-
burg (Rendsborg). 
Although Rendsburg’s importance for the Danish king grew during the 18th century up until the Danish defeat in the Second 
Schleswig War in 1864, the Mint was only functional for four years during the reign of Frederik IV . 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
