Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Stunning Near Gem Ducat Struck from Gold from Imperial China
13219
DENMARK. Ducat, 1746-A. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-64. Fr-254; KM-548; Hede-3A; Sieg-24.1; 
Schou-8; Bruun-7607. Weight: 3.51 gms. Mintmaster: I. M. Wineke. Engraver: Magnus Gustavus Arbien. A tremen -
dous nearly-Gem Ducat, this entrancing piece offers a vibrant luster and handsome nearly prooflike appeal, with nicely 
frosted well struck details. The fields are watery with reflectivity, and no evident strafe or mark mars the surfaces. A piece 
that is deserving of the utmost attention from any collectors seeking to acquire a rarity for their numismatic cabinets.
As the text, EX AURO SINICO (“From Chinese gold”) reveals, this type was stuck from Chinese gold traded for silver by 
the Danish Asiatic Company acting under protection by the king (REGALIBUS AUSPICIIS). The Danish Asiatic Company 
was the successor to the Danish East India Company founded in 1730, and began trade with Qing China at Canton. Travel-
ing through the Danish outposts on the Indian Subcontinent, the Danish Asiatic Company found little success and lost its 
monopoly on Danish trade with the Orient in 1772. All former outposts became crown colonies a few years later, ending the 
brief run of the Company.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: C. F. Schubart (Copenhagen - 1831) Lot # 2063.

========== PAGE 160 ==========

Page 134 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Extremely Impressive Ducat Struck from Chinese Gold
13220
DENMARK. Ducat, 1746-A. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-64. Fr-259; KM-551; Hede-5B; Sieg-26.2; 
Schou-12; Bruun-7611. Weight: 3.49 gms. Mintmaster: I. M. Wineke. Engraver: Magnus Gustavus Arbien. Beautiful 
and historically interesting Ducat struck, as the reverse exergue reveals, with gold brought home from trades with China.
€10,000-€15,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 2463 in his collection.

========== PAGE 165 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 139
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Dazzling Superb Gem Birthday Ducat
13235
DENMARK. 12 Mark (Courant Ducat), 1771-K. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-66. Fr-280; KM-624; 
Hede-3; Sieg-26; Schou-2; FP-E7; Bruun-7756. Weight: 3.11 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Schivern Knoph. Engraver: Johan 
Ephraim Bauert. A superb Gem Uncirculated example of spectacular beauty. 
This beautiful one-year type was struck in only 131 pieces for the king’s birthday on the 29th of January. Most were used as 
largesse money at the riding court at Christiansborg Castle. 
The issue seems to have been instigated by the minister Johann Friedrich Struensee, who was also the mentally ill king’s phy-
sician. The following year Struensee was arrested, quartered and decapitated for having an affair with the Queen, Caroline 
Mathilde.
€18,000-€22,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Extremely Rare Wildman Ducat with Exceptional Luster
13236
DENMARK. Ducat, 1771. Altona Mint. Christian VII. NGC Unc Details—Obverse Graffiti. Fr-282; KM-625; Hede-
1; Sieg-28; Schou-1; FP-46.1; Lange 122; Bruun-7779. Weight: 3.48 gms. Mintmaster: Caspar Henrik Wolff. Engraver: 
Johan Henrik Wolff after a design by Johannes Wiedewelt. Extremely rare and preserving remarkable luster highly 
unusual for the type.
Only 341 examples were struck for Baron von Schimmelmann in Hamburg with 10 examples given to Count J. F. Struensee. 
The legend MON AUR ALTONA V ADLEGEM IMPERII (“Gold coin from Altona according to the law of the Empire”) and 
the reverse design resembling the popular Dutch trade Ducats indicate that it was struck for international trade.
€10,000-€15,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Highly Desirable Presentation Off-Metal Strike in Gold
13239
DENMARK. Gold Off-Metal Strike 2 Skilling, 1778-CHL. Altona Mint. Christian VII. NGC Unc Details—Reverse 
Scratched. Fr-unlisted; KM-631.2; Hede-33B; Sieg-4.2; Schou-13a; FP-7.3; Bruun-7802. Weight: 3.39 gms. Mintmaster: 
Caspar Henrich Lyng. Engraver: Johan Henrik Wolff. Apart from a few superficial marks in the obverse field, an abso-
lutely choice example of an excessively rare and largely unlisted type struck on a gold planchet with the approximate 
weight of a Ducat. 
The reason behind this off-strike in gold is speculative, but the perfect strike combined with the fact that 1778 is the first date 
of a new type, could indicate that the present piece functioned as a presentation piece. This hypothesis is further strengthened 
by the fact that it was struck on a specially made flan, and not just for instance on a Danish Courant Ducat as the corre-
sponding Norwegian 2 Skillings (see for instance, OM 17, 1986, lot 263 and lot 265).
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Mintmaster: Didrik Christian Lyng. Engraver: Johan Henrik 
Wolff. This the last date of the Courant Ducats, which had been 
minted throughout the 18th century. It must have been one of 
the very last to be minted, as the die is both cracked and has 
many imperfections, most noticeably on the portrait.

Excellent Choice Mint State Wildman Taler
13242
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1786. Altona / Poppenbüttel Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-63. Dav-1310; KM-640.1; 
Hede-22B; Sieg-18.2; Schou-1; FP-34.2; Lange- ; Bruun-7863. Weight: 28.02 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Flor. Engraver: 
Johan Henrik Wolff. Excellent condition for type.
These Speciedalers take their imagery from the Dutch Albertstalers which had become incredibly popular as trade coinage 
in the Baltic in the late 18th century. Also known as ‘ Albertsdalere’ , these Danish versions come with four different dates.
€3,500-€4,500

Delightfully Toned Prooflike Travel Taler
13248
DENMARK. Kurantdaler, 1788-DIA/MF. Altona/Poppenbüttel Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-64 Prooflike. Dav-
1312; KM-645; Hede-25; Sieg-19; NMD-1; Schou-7; FP-E5; Bruun-7893. Weight: 23.58 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Flor. 
Engraver: Daniel Jensen Adzer. Proof Like luster and delightful blue and yellow toning hues combine to make this 
spectacular example stand tall among all known examples of this highly coveted type struck in 5,000 specimens for the 
Crown Prince Frederik’s travel to Norway in June 1788.
The beautiful type was created by the highly competent royal medalist, Adzer, who with understandable pride signed his 
work (D.I.A.) on both the obverse and the reverse. It bears the rather pompous inscription: UROKKELIG SOM DOVRES 
HØYE FIELDE STAAER NORGES SØNNERS TROSKAB MOD OG VÆLDE (“The sons of Norway’s allegiance, courage 
and might are as adamant as the high mountains of Dovre”). This is the oldest Danish / Norwegian type where the king’s 
name and title have been written in Danish and not in Latin.
€10,000-€15,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 172 ==========

Page 146 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Insanely Impressive 20 Ducats of the Utmost Importance and Rarity
13249
DENMARK. 20 Ducats, 1788-DIA/MF . Altona / Poppenbüttel Mint. Christian VII. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. 
Off-strike in gold of Rigsdaler Courant struck for the personal use of the king and the crown prince. Fr-unlisted; KM-
unlisted (cf. 645 in silver); Hede-25; Sieg-19; Schou-7a; FP-E5 and p. 338; Bruun-7894. Weight: 69.22 gms. Mintmaster: 
Michael Flor. Engraver: Daniel Jensen Adzer. The largest of all Danish / Norwegian gold coins ever minted and unde-
niably one of the most desirable of all in the L. E. Bruun collection. A magnific example of a truly imposing piece that 
will become the jewel in the crown of any collection of coins from the dual monarchy of Denmark-Norway. Unique in 
private hands. Only known in three examples, the present and two publicly owned examples in Trondheim and Oslo. 
That the only two other existing examples rest in public collections in Norway points to the fact that this hefty gold piece were 
intended for presents / awards to especially important dignitaries whom the Crown Prince met on his visit to Norway. It is 
also quite likely that this particular example has in fact been in royal possession since it was first made. It was bequeathed 
to counsellor of state, Julius Gustav Berger Klemp (1813-1890), who had acted as executor of king Frederik VII’s estate by 
his widow, countess Louise Danner.
€250,000-€350,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: J. G. B. Klemp Collection (Copenhagen - 1891) Lot # 397.


========== PAGE 173 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 147

13250
DENMARK. Dukat, 1791. Altona Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-67. Fr-283; KM-650; Hede-2; Sieg-29; Schou-1; 
FP-47.1; Bruun-7910. Weight: 3.48 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Flor. Engraver: Georg Valentin Bauert. The so-called 
“Wildman Ducat” was the last Danish Ducat produced and the only one which gives the exact weight and fineness in the 
design, suiting it for international trade. Exceptionally well preserved.

========== PAGE 174 ==========

Page 148 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
    
13252
DENMARK. Schleswig-Holstein. Silver Speciedaler Pattern 
(Restrike), 1799-M/MF. Altona Mint. Christian VII. NGC 
PROOF-62. KM-Pn3c; Hede-39F; Sieg-53; Schou-6; Bru -
un-7945. Weight: 27.44 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Flor. Engrav-
er: John Milton. Wonderfully toned proof restrike.
The original patterns of 1799, 1800, and ND were all engraved in 
London by either Milton or the Danish engraver Bauert, who was 
in London in connection with the Danish Royal Mint considering 
buying a Boulton Press. The patterns themselves were struck both 
in London and in Copenhagen. Around 1890 L. E. Bruun com -
missioned a number of restrikes of Danish medals from the Royal 
Mint, and it is likely that this and the other pattern restrikes were 
commissioned at the same time. It is possible, however, that the 
restrikes are much older. The Royal Mint continued selling restrikes 
up until 1971; from 1915 with an ‘N’ (for ‘Nypræg’-restrike) on 
the rim. 
€350-€400
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
13253
DENMARK. Schleswig-Holstein. Gold Off-Metal Strike 1/24 
Speciedaler/2 1/2 Schilling, 1801-MF. Altona Mint. Christian 
VII. NGC Unc Details—Tooled. KM-124 (silver); Hede-44 (sil-
ver); Sieg-40 (silver); Schou-9a; FP-SH4.5; Bruun-7972. Weight: 
3.49 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Flor. Engraver: Georg Valentin 
Bauert. Ducat off-strike in gold. A rare and remarkable piece, 
this gold pattern was struck to the weight of a Ducat. 
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

13256
DENMARK. Silver 1/3 Speciedaler Pattern, ND (1808). Co-
penhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC PROOF-65 Cameo. 
Hede-18; Sieg-48; FP-P3; Schou-5; Bruun-7990. Weight: 9.99 
gms. Pattern struck in Copenhagen from dies made at the Soho 
Mint in Birmingham. Engraver: Conrad H. Küchler. Somewhat 
later restrike with plain edge. Exceptional surfaces and most 
wonderful cameo. 
€1,200-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 175 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 149
Session I • Friday , March 14, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
13257
DENMARK. Silver 1/6 Speciedaler Pattern, ND (1808). Co-
penhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC PROOF-65 Cameo. 
Hede-19; Sieg-47; FP-P3; Schou-6; Bruun-7993. Weight: 5.25 
gms. Pattern struck in Copenhagen from dies made at the Soho 
Mint in Birmingham Engraver: Conrad H. Küchler. Somewhat 
later restrike with plain edge. Amazing condition; the photos 
cannot capture the beauty of this piece which deserves to be seen 
in person. 
€1,200-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
13258
DENMARK. Silver 1/12 Speciedaler Pattern, ND (1808). Co-
penhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC PROOF-65. Hede-20; 
Sieg-46; FP-P3; Schou-7; Bruun-7995. Weight: 2.91 gms. Pat -
tern struck in Copenhagen from dies made at the Soho Mint 
in Birmingham. Engraver: Conrad H. Küchler. Somewhat later 
restrike with plain edge. Wonderful toning on this exceptional 
Proof-65 pattern. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
13259
DENMARK. Silver 2 Skilling Pattern, 1811-IC. Copenhagen 
Mint. Frederik VI. NGC MS-62. KM-cf. 670; Hede-9; Sieg-4; 
Schou-2a; Bruun-8010. Weight: 4.46 gms. Extremely rare silver 
pattern which very rarely comes on the market; the last was sold 
in public auction in 2008. A wonderful Mint State example with 
attractive toning. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
13260
DENMARK. 12 Skilling, 1812-IC. Copenhagen Mint. Freder-
ik VI. NGC MS-64 Red. KM-673; Hede-11; Sieg-6.1; Schou-3; 
Bruun-8014. Mintmaster: Johannes Conradsen (IC). Struck on a 
Skilling 1771, KM-616 (note the inflation from 1 to 12 Skilling). 
The condition of this coin is breathtaking; a real showstopper.
The 1771 Skillings were recalled in 1812 and many were restruck 
as 12 Skillings. On this piece it is especially visible on the obverse.  
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
13261
DENMARK. Lauenburg. 2/3 Taler (Zweidrittel), 1830-FA/FF. 
Altona Mint. Frederik VI. NGC MS-61. KM-25; Hede-1; Sieg-
44; Schou-3; Bruun-8082. Mintmaster: Johan Friedrich Freund. 
Engraver: Frederik Alsing. Although minted for use in Lauen -
burg (Lauenborg) this type was struck in Altona in a number of 
only 4,051 pieces. Excellent Mint State example of the only Dan-
ish emission from the small duchy on the borders of Schleswig-
Holstein and Mecklenburg-Schwerin.
Lauenburg ended up in Danish hands after the Napoleonic Wars. 
As part of the agreement on ceding Norway to Sweden, Denmark 
got Swedish Pomerania in return. That was quickly traded with 
Prussia for Lauenburg. Denmark kept Lauenburg until the 2nd 
Schleswig War when Prussia conquered it along with Schleswig-
Holstein. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 176 ==========

Page 150 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
    
13262
DENMARK. 16 Reichsbankschilling, 1831-IFF. Altona Mint. Frederik VI. NGC MS-65. KM-154a; Hede-30C; Sieg-
24; Schou-2; Bruun-8084. Weight: 4.30 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Friedrich Freund. Engraver: Hans Frederik Alsing. 
Amazing condition on this smaller silver denomination, very rare in this pristine condition. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Extremely Rare and Historically Interesting Pattern
13263
DENMARK. Provisional Government in Schleswig-Holstein. Silver Schilling Pattern, 1851. Altona (Copenhagen) 
Mint. Frederik VII. NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. KM-unlisted; Hede-18; Sieg-23; Schou-4; AKS-12; Lange - ; Bru -
un-8206. Weight: 2.40 gms. Mintmaster: Theodor C. W . Andersen. Engraver: Carl Heinrich Lorenz. Excessively rare 
contemporary Pattern. 
Whereas relatively large quantities of Sechslings and Dreilings were minted in copper, the Schillings were never minted before 
the insurrection had been struck down by the Danish Army which brought back the dies as booty and had 5 examples made, 
of which this is one.
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
End of Session 1

COINS OF NORW AY
One of Only Three in Private Hands
The Best known Specimen, Private or Public
14001
NORW AY . Hvid, ND (1523-33). Bergen Mint. Frederik I. NGC MS-62. Galster-171; Sieg-1; NMD-9; Schou-36; Bru-
un-9282. Weight: 0.76 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Hansen (?). Perfectly struck, nicely toned and even retaining a bit of 
original luster. An almost impossibly well-preserved coin and easily the best in existence. Excessively rare with only a 
further two in private hands.
€10,000-€15,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 214 in his collection.

========== PAGE 179 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 153
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
14003
NORW AY . Skilling, 1535. Oslo Mint. Christian III. NGC VF-
30. Galster-175; Sieg-1; NMD-1; Schou-1; Bruun-9290. Weight: 
1.44 gms. A very rare and historically interesting issue minted for 
Christian as pretender to the throne (HERES NORVEGIE) by 
the lord of Akershus Castle, Erik Gyldenstierne. 
€4,500-€5,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Bruun owned two examples of this coin and it is no longer possible to 
tell if the present example was formerly in the Joh. G. Guildal Collection 
(# 229) or in the Countship of Brahesminde Collection.
   
14004
NORW AY . Skilling, ND. Nidaros (Trondheim) Mint. Olav 
Engelbrektsson. NGC EF-40. Galster-187; Sieg-1; NMD-2; 
Schou-5; Bruun-9295. Weight: 2.12 gms. Mintmaster: Jacob 
Schult. An exquisite example of this interesting issue struck for 
the last Norwegian Archbishop, Olav Engelbrektsson. 
Following his defeat against the Danish protestant king, Christian 
III, Engelbrektsson fled to Belgium bringing with him a number of 
valuable treasures from the Nidaros Cathedral. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Exceedingly Rare Emergency Klippe
14002
NORW AY . 2 Skilling Klippe, ND (1531-32). Oslo Mint. Christian II. NGC EF-45. Galster-174; Sieg-1A; NMD-5; 
Schou-1; Schive XV .14; Bruun-9285. Weight: 1.33 gms. An amazingly detailed and perfectly centered example of this 
exceedingly rare and historically very interesting emergency issue minted by Christian II during his failed attempt to 
regain power.
Oslo was renamed Christiania in 1624 in honor of King Christian IV . It reverted to the name Oslo in 1925.
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

========== PAGE 180 ==========

Page 154 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Attractive Early Norwegian Skilling
Only a Handful Known in Private Collections
14005
NORW AY . Skilling, 1537. Bergen Mint. Christian III. NGC EF-45. Galster-176; Sieg-1; NMD-1A; Schou-21; Bru-
un-9298. Weight: 1.21 gms. A most attractive example with deep, warm toning. 
The obverse legend: CRISTIANI DIE GRA REX NORVIE is very interesting as this is the last time Norway is numismatically 
recognized as a sovereign state and not just a part of the Danish realm.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: H. H. J. Lynge Collection (Copenhagen - 1899) Lot # 285.
HERMAN HENRIK JULIUS LYNGE  
(1822-1897) 
Herman Henrik Julius Lynge (1822-1897) is widely con-
sidered to be the father of the professional antiquarian 
book trade in Denmark. He obtained both the Danish 
and the Swedish medal of honor, and when he turned 70, 
he was awarded the title of "Kancelliråd" (titulary chan -
cellor). His estate auction took place in 1899 and was di-
vided into 5 small catalogs comprising a total of several 
thousand coins, medals, tokens and paper money.


========== PAGE 181 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 155
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Extremely Rare Mark with Excellent Toning
Best of Only Three in Private Collections
14006
NORW AY . Mark, 1546. Gimsøy Mint. Christian III. NGC EF-40. Hede-2; Sieg-3; NMD-6; Schou-4; Bruun-9303. 
Weight: 9.66 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock, the younger. Lovely and intense, rainbow-toning makes this the most at-
tractive of the three known privately held examples (the other two being the Bille-Brahe example sold to L. E. Bruun and 
resold in the 1925 auction of duplicates as lot # 1634, and the Zinck example (Zinck VI, 2003, lot # 313).
This important but highly elusive type was struck at Gimsøy in the former nunnery, near Skien in Telemark county, presum-
ably from local silver mined at Gullnes. The mint was only in use from 1544 to 1546 when the buildings burned down and 
the silver mine in Gullnes dried out.
€30,000-€40,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: H. H. J. Lynge Collection (Copenhagen - 1899) Lot # 306.
Ex: Alfred Benzon Collection (Copenhagen - 1887) Lot # 70.

========== PAGE 182 ==========

Page 156 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Very Rare and Excellent for Type
14007
NORW AY . Skilling, 1546. Gimsøy Mint. Christian III. NGC EF-45. Hede-4; Sieg-1; NMD-9; Schou-11; Bruun-9305. 
Weight: 1.96 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock, the younger. An extremely presentable example of this highly elusive type 
which is notoriously difficult to obtain in presentable condition.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
14008
NORW AY . Speciedaler, 1629. Christiania Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Reverse Damage. Dav-3529; KM-
8; Hede-1; Sieg-15.1; NMD-28A; Schou-8; Bruun-9317. Weight: 25.72 gms. Mintmaster: Anders Pedersen. A distin -
guished example of a rare date. 
The Mint in Christiania (present day Oslo) was opened in 1628 and the goldsmith, Anders Pedersen was appointed mintmas-
ter on the 28th of April. This and all the following Specie denominations struck for Christian IV carry the following bible quote 
(Proverbs 10:22): BENEDICTIO DOMINI DIVITES FACIT (“The blessing of the Lord gives wealth”), referring to the rich 
silver finds at Kongsberg. The design rather closely imitates the first Norwegian Taler from 1546 minted at Gimsøy, and this 
combination of a portrait of the king and the axe-bearing lion became a standard for all future Talers minted at Christiania. 
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

========== PAGE 183 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 157
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT

14009
NORW AY . 1/2 Speciedaler, 1629. Christiania Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Brushed. KM-7; Hede-2; Sieg-
12; NMD-51; Schou-18; Bruun-9320. Weight: 12.79 gms. Mintmaster: Anders Pedersen. A wonderfully detailed ex -
ample of this excessively rare date which is otherwise only known in public collections (one in Copenhagen and two in 
Oslo).
€20,000-€25,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 855 in his collection.

========== PAGE 197 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 171
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
A Most Magnificent Combination of Preservation and Toning
14028
NORW AY . Speciedaler, 1646-PG. Christiania Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-63. Dav-3534; KM-12; Hede-5B; Sieg-
16.2; NMD-47; Schou-29; Bruun-9533. Weight: 28.99 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Grüner. Immensely lustrous, seducingly 
toned and exceptionally detailed. 
€10,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Alfred Benzon Collection (Copenhagen - 1887) Lot # 803.
    
14029
NORW AY . Mark, 1646. Christiania Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-55. KM-27; Hede-20; Sieg-6; NMD-119; Aagaard-
TC2; Schou-63; Bruun-9544. Weight: 5.23 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Grüner. Wonderfully toned and unusually well-
struck example of a scarcer date. 
In the middle of the Torstenson War and possibly to invoke the help of God against the Swedes, King Christian personally 
sketched the coin design using the Tetragrammaton (the Hebrew four-letter-name of God) as a key element on silver and 
gold coins struck in Copenhagen, Christiania, and Glückstadt 
€1,200-€1,800
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Extremely Rare Hefty Triple Taler
The Only Known Example in Private Hands
Unique Die Combination
14034
NORW AY . 3 Speciedaler, 1649-PG. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-3586; KM-A39; 
Hede-10A; Sieg-23.1; NMD-16B; Schou-2; Aagaard-19.1 (49-3/49-16); Bruun-9591. Weight: 81.02 gms. Mintmaster: 
Peter Grüner. Engraver: Hans zum Busch. In spite of some surface marks, a very impressive and nicely struck example 
of this hefty issue. All Norwegian Triple Talers from the reign of Frederik III rank among the legendary rarities, and of 
the three registered examples from 1649, this is the only one in private hands, and the only known struck from this die 
combination.
€90,000-€120,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Frederik Chr. Bech Collection (Copenhagen - 1906) Lot # 426.

Magnificent Triple Taler
Unique in Private Hands, Only Four Known in Total
14044
NORW AY . 3 Speciedaler, 1654-FG. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Edge Filing, Tooled. Dav-3593; 
KM-B39a; Hede-19; Sieg-28; NMD-21; Aagaard-43.1 (53-7/54-1); Schou-2; Bruun-9678. Weight: 85.92 gms. Mintmas-
ter: Frederik Grüner. Engraver: Jeremias Hercules (obverse). The 1654 Triple Taler is known by only four examples, all 
of them struck from a different combination of dies. The remaining three examples are located in the National Museum 
of Denmark and in the collection at Lund University, making this amazing, majestic coin unique in private ownership.
€100,000-€150,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Peter v. Hemmert Collection (Copenhagen - 1807) Lot # 823.

========== PAGE 211 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 185
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
14047
NORW AY . 2 Mark, 1654. Christiania Mint. Archbishopric of Salzburg countermark. Frederik III. NGC EF De -
tails—Obverse Scratched. KM-33; Hede-67B; Sieg-6.2; NMD-154; Schou-34; Bruun-9688. Weight: 10.70 gms. Mint-
master: Frederik Grüner. The obverse right field bears a clearly struck oval countermark from the Archbishopric of 
Salzburg, extremely rare thus. 
In 1681 the Archbishop of Salzburg, Max Gandolph of Kuenberg instructed that all foreign silver coins should be counter-
marked for a charge and the value lowered as many of the circulating coins were deemed base.
(For another example of countermarked coinage from Salzburg, see lot# 13163). 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
14048
NORW AY . Speciedaler, 1655-FG. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-58. Dav-3595; cf. KM-48; Hede-17; Sieg-
26; NMD-77; Aagaard-45.7 (54-3/55-5); Schou-10; Bruun-9696. Weight: 28.63 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. En-
graver: Jeremias Hercules (obverse). A minute edge-split in the flan doesn’t detract from the overall excellent eye-appeal 
of this attractively toned Taler. 
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

JOHAN ANDREAS FERDINAND WOLFF (1814-1893)
Johan Andreas Ferdinand Wolff (1814-1893) was a bank director (Bikuben), 
merchant, and Danish consul in Manila. His collection was cataloged by Peter 
Hauberg (later to become the managing director of the Royal Coin Cabinet) 
and sold in 1882.

Majestic Double Taler
Unique in Private Hands
14059
NORW AY . 2 Speciedaler, 1662-FG. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. Dav-3610; KM-89; 
Hede-42; Sieg-47; NMD-54; Aagaard-78.2; Schou-18; Bruun-9887. Weight: 57.46 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. 
Engraver: Johan Blum (obverse). A ravishing example with an abundance of luster and a wonderful strike befitting of 
this majestic piece. Unique in private hands with only two more examples registered in the national collections of Den-
mark and Norway. The obverse was engraved in Copenhagen by the medalist Johan Blum and is widely considered one 
of his most successful works of all. The dignified portrait is larger than usual, eminently balanced and with a bonanza 
of details.
€60,000-€80,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: C. F. Schubart (Copenhagen - 1831) Lot # 1194.

14065
NORW AY . Speciedaler, 1665-FG. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-cf. 3619; KM-cf. 
59; Hede-53D; Sieg-56.4; NMD-108; Aagaard-103.1; Schou-26; Bruun-9959. Weight: 28.66 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik 
Grüner. A glorious portrait type with sublime details. Although at some point lightly cleaned, this coin has excellently 
re-toned with speckles of blue. Unique with no other examples being registered in neither public nor private collections. 
Although somewhat speculative, the fact that Bruun acquired the coin at the Peter von Hemmert sale might indicate a 
previous royal provenance.
€20,000-€30,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Peter v. Hemmert Collection (Copenhagen - 1807) Lot # 972.

NORW AY . 4 Mark (Krone), 1669-FG. Christiania Mint. 
Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-3662; KM-un-

rare date. This type was copied by contemporary counterfeit -
ers and Sven Aagaard has convincingly argued that the originals 
(such as this) are far scarcer than the counterfeits, the so-called 
‘Mystery Krone’ .

Great Rarity and Great Condition Combined
14073
NORW AY . Speciedaler, 1670-FG. Christiania Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-62. Dav-3650A; KM-98; Hede-25B; Sieg-
49.2; NMD-55; Schou-11; Bruun-10088. Weight: 28.83 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. A supremely noble example 
with a sublime luster. This excessively rare type is known in only a handful examples. 
Grüner’s very unusual crowned portrait is not only exceptionally beautiful but also historically significant as Christian V 
was the first Danish-Norwegian monarch to ascend the throne by divine right as an absolute monarch explaining why he 
could be shown wearing a crown before his actual anointing on June 7th, 1671.
€45,000-€65,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Lauritz C. Petersen Collection (Copenhagen - 1914) Lot # 1056.

The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Unbelievably Rare TRIPLE Ducat of the Highest Grade
Gorgeous Elephant Type
14078
NORW AY . 3 Ducats, 1673. Christiania Mint. Christian V . NGC AU-58. Fr-13; KM-127; Hede-15; Sieg-79; NMD-6A; 
Schou-1; Bruun-10143. Weight: 9.89 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. A wonderfully detailed and lustrous example 
of a highly coveted and charming type. No matter the date, Norwegian gold coins larger than a Double Ducat are con-
sidered completely unobtainable.
Although the statutes, which are still valid, were not written until 1693, the tower-bearing elephant had since the late 
middle-ages been a part of Denmark’s oldest and most distinguished royal order of chivalry.
€150,000-€250,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Peter v. Hemmert Collection (Copenhagen - 1807) Lot # 123.

ter Grüner, the younger. This beautiful and unusual one-year

14090

Unique in Private Hands
14092
NORW AY . Speciedaler, 1690-HCM. Kongsberg Mint. Christian V . NGC AU-50. Dav-3658; KM-184; Hede-37B; Sieg-
58; NMD-167; Schou-1; Bruun-10372. Weight: 28.97 gms. Mintmaster: Henning Christopher Meyer, the elder. The edge 
bears the following lettering: HÆC BOREAS CYMBRO FERT ORNAMENTO LABORUM (“Such wonderful fruits of 
labor brings the Northern wind to the Danes”). A high-caliber example with russet hues bringing forth the very unique 
portrait in the best possible way. 
The earliest Norwegian Talers with a lettered edge (Hede-37A and 37B) are all insanely rare with only 2 privately owned 
examples in total from all three dates (1687, 1688, 1690).
€15,000-€25,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Ole Devegge Collection (Copenhagen - 1851) Lot # 3406.

========== PAGE 235 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 209
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Very Highly Graded, Edge Lettered Speciedaler
14093
NORW AY . Speciedaler, 1692-HCM. Kongsberg Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-62. Dav-3659A; KM-190; Hede-37C; 
Sieg-59.1; NMD-169A; Schou-9; Bruun-10390. Mintmaster: Henning Christopher Meyer, the elder. The edge bears 
the following lettering: HÆC BOREAS CYMBRO FERT ORNAMENTO LABORUM (“Such wonderful fruits of labor 
brings the Northern wind to the Danes”). An unmistakably superior example of this ever popular type.
By far most of the Speciedalers struck in Denmark/Norway in the 17th century were not made for regular circulation but for 
use as presentation pieces or savings. The Norwegian Speciedalers with lettered edges are however notable exceptions, as a 
total of almost 100,000 is known to have been minted from 1692-1696. As opposed to being hammer struck, the Kongsberg 
Speciedalers were minted in a screw press after the lettering had already been applied to the planchets, which means that the 
reading direction and the starting point of the legends vary. There are seven known varieties of edge legends of which five are in 
Danish, one is in German and one is in Latin. They were all meant to glorify the Danish absolute monarch and celebrate the 
important occurrence of silver in the Norwegian cliffs around the new mint in Kongsberg that had taken over from Christiania 
in 1686. The phrases, which are extraordinarily high-flown and poetical, are believed to be by the vicar Jørgen Kongstad.
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
14094
NORW AY . Mark, 1692-HCM. Kongsberg Mint. Christian V . NGC AU-58. KM-185.2; Hede-75; Sieg-27; NMD-218A; 
Schou-42; Bruun-10399. Weight: 5.41 gms. Mintmaster: Henning Christopher Meyer, the elder. Pleasantly toned and 
sharply struck. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Alfred Benzon Collection (Copenhagen - 1887) Lot # 1899.

Attractive Double Ducat
Very Few Known in Private Ownership
14101
NORW AY . 2 Ducat, ND. Christiania Mint. Christian V . NGC AU-58. Fr-11; KM-120; Hede-8; Sieg-67; NMD-11A; 
Schou-15; Bruun-10498. Weight: 6.92 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Grüner. A handsome portrait combined with tantalizing 
luster surrounding the letters make this a highly desirable example. Excessively rare with less than a handful known to 
be in private hands. Struck with the same obverse die as the Danish 1/4 Ducat (III Marck) 1676 (KM-360, Hede-9), but 
on a very thick planchet.

========== PAGE 239 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 213
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
One of the Best Extant of this Extremely Rare and Most Popular Type
14102
NORW AY . Ducat, ND. Christiania Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-62. Fr-189 (Denmark); KM-unlisted; Hede-19B; Sieg-
81.2; NMD-22A; Schou-35; Bruun-10504. Weight: 3.88 gms. Excessively rare and easily one of the very best of the few 
in private hands. 
One of the most beloved of all Norwegian coins featuring a reverse where the Norwegian lion is placing its paw on the 
shoulder of a man in full armor with the visor down. Due to a similarity with a soldier’s badge from 1674 (Galster-221) 
this amusing type is presumed to have been struck for the Norwegian Governor-general, the illegitimate son of Frederik III, 
Ulrik Frederik Gyldenløve.
€20,000-€30,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

========== PAGE 240 ==========

Page 214 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Gorgeously Toned Silver Off-Metal Strike
14103

Gorgeous and Highly Interesting Gold Off-Metal Strike
Extremely Rare With Only About a Handful Known
14110
NORW AY . Gold Off-Metal Strike 8 Skilling, 1733-NBF. Kongsberg Mint. Christian VI. NGC AU Details--Dam-
aged. Fr-unlisted; KM-224 (silver); Hede-2 (silver); Sieg-2 (silver); NMD-21 (silver); Schou-5a (gold); Bruun-10670. 
Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolai Bernhard Fuchs. Engraver: Erasmus Simon Kongsberg. Highly interesting off-
strike on a Dutch Ducat. The purpose and the size of this mintage is not known. Although excessively rare today with 
only a handful of examples known (two examples were formerly in the collection of Helge Reff, see OM 17, 1986, lot 222 
and 18, 1987, lot 413), it seems clear that it was not struck at random.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

14111
NORW AY . 4 Mark (Krone), 1736-C. Kongsberg Mint. Christian VI. NGC AU-53. Dav-1296; KM-233; Hede-1; Sieg-
5; NMD-3; Schou-1; Bruun-10682. Weight: 22.33 gms. Mintmaster: Government Commission. Good condition for this 
rare one-year type.
The Mintmaster at Kongsberg, Nicolai Bernhard Fuchs, was suspended 1735 suspected of fraud. From 1735 to 1737 the mint 
was run by a commission, hence the ‘C’ mintmark. 
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

is difficult to distinguish any details from an earlier strike, the weight indicates that this excessively rare and interesting 
piece was most likely struck on a French Louis d’ or.

Lovely Toned and Very Rare Gold Off-Metal Strike
Struck on a French Louis d’ or
14113
NORW AY . Gold Off-Metal Strike 24 Skilling, 1760-TL. Kongsberg Mint. Frederik V . NGC Unc Details--Edge Fil-
ing. Fr-unlisted; KM-236 (silver); Hede-2A (silver); Sieg-3.1 (silver); NMD-17 (silver); Schou-3a (gold); Bruun-10742. 
Weight: 8.11 gms. Mintmaster: Truls Andersen Lyng. Engraver: Gabriel Andersen Lunde. Gold off-strike on a French 
Louis d’ or. Very lustrous and beautifully struck. Of the utmost rarity. 
The fact that this coin was struck on a French gold coin with a weight that does not correlate with the Danish currency has 
made a domestic use impossible, so either it must have been ordered struck to be used in an international payment or more 
likely as a kind of gold bar.
€8,000-€10,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
COINS OF SWEDEN
SWEDEN’S POSSESSIONS IN EUROPE
The Bruun collection contains a great number of Swedish coins, 
the vast majority of which comes from the Countship of Brahes-
minde Collection purchased by L. E. Bruun in 1922. Many of the 
Swedish coins are struck in cities more than 1,000 kilometres 
(621 miles) away from the borders of 17th century Sweden. These 
are the coins of the Swedish Possessions in Europe.
Sweden became independent from Denmark in the early 16th 
century when the Kalmar Union of 1396 finally collapsed, and 
Gustav Vasa claimed the throne.
Since the Viking Age the Swedes had mainly sought their fortune 
in the East, and therefore it was natural for them to expand their 
realm into the Baltic States in the late 16th century. Narva, Reval 
(present day Tallinn) and Riga all fell under Swedish rule around 
1600. This happened somewhat voluntarily: the region was under 
pressure from the expanding Russian Empire and of the two nations, Sweden was the preferred choice.
Simultaneously, a grandson of Gustav Vasa, Sigismund III Vasa, became king of Poland (and 
for a while also Sweden) and that made it even more natural for Sweden to look to the south-
east and south, blocked as it was by Denmark/Norway to the west. 
The European Thirty Y ears’ War (1618-1648) saw both Sweden and Denmark enter as protec-
tors of the Protestant cause. While the Danish participation under Christian IV was short-
lived (1625-1629) and catastrophic (it was nearly the end of Denmark as an independent na-
tion), the Swedes under king Gustav II Adolf (Gustavus Adolphus) quickly became a deciding 
force.  Having already conquered the important Hanseatic city of Elbing (present day Elblag, 
Poland) in 1626, Sweden joined the Thirty Y ears’ War in 1630 and gained a succession of vic-
tories. Stralsund and Stettin (Szczecin) also fell to the Swedes, who held Pomerania (or parts 
thereof) until 1814. Many of the gold and larger silver coins from those cities found in the L. E. 
Bruun Collection are often in far better condition than what is normally seen on the market.
The death of King Gustav II Adolf on the battlefield at Lützen in 1632 did not stop the Swedes. 
Cities fell to their army as it ploughed its way through Germany and in many of the cities, coins 
were struck in the name of the King – even posthumously; both because of the symbol he had 
become but also because his heir was then a six-year-old girl, Kristina. 
In Southern Germany, Swedish coinage was struck within the conquered cities or in travelling mints in the military camps 
outside Nürnberg (Nuremberg), Mainz, Augsburg, Fürth, and Würzburg. These coins were often struck with silver and gold 
looted from the cities to pay the troops, and the coins themselves were engraved and struck by local craftsmen, giving the 
coinage a diverse and distinctly foreign (relative to the Swedes) air. 
While the Swedes did not hold the aforementioned cities for more 
than a few years and a treaty with France was signed in 1635, which 
ended the war for Sweden, they remained in Germany and contin-
ued to make their presence felt. At the Peace of Westphalia of 1648, 
the Swedish general and Lord High Chancellor, Axel Oxenstierna 
was one of its key architects.
Other Swedish possessions included the Bishopric of Bremen and 
Verden, which they held from the Peace of Westphalia until the 
Peace in Sntockholm in 1719, when it was given to Duke Georg of 
Hanover (who also happened to be king George I of England) and 
the city of Wismar, also granted in 1648 and held until 1803.
The Great Northern War of 1700-1721 saw basically all of Sweden’s 
neighbouring countries ganging up on her to contest her suprem-
Sigismund III Vasa


========== PAGE 250 ==========

Page 224 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
acy. Even then, Sweden almost managed to come out on top, winning many battles (and forcing Denmark to withdraw as 
early as August 1700). The outcome of the war meant Sweden lost its possessions in the Baltic States to Russia, most of its 
possessions in Northern Germany / Poland to Prussia and Hanover, and Holstein-Gottorp came back under Danish control. 
Sweden did keep Finland, which it had held since medieval times. It was, however, lost to the Russians in 1809. 
In 1720 the Swedish Queen, Ulrika Eleonora, abdicated. The daughter of Karl XI and sister of Karl XII, she inherited the 
throne on her brother’s death in 1718, but after two years she gave up her throne in favour of her husband, Fredrik I. Fredrik 
was the son of the Landgraf of Hesse-Kassel, and upon his father’s death he became Landgraf of Hesse-Kassel as well as king 
of Sweden. The coins of Fredrik (‘Friedrich’ in German), both the Swedish and the Hessian, were placed under Sweden in the 
1928 Bruun catalogue. As these coins were issued in Germany by a German ruler (albeit also king of Sweden), and since we 
are abiding by the 1928 Bruun catalog order, you will see coins labelled ‘Germany’ within the Swedish section. Similarly, you 
will find Norwegian coins within the Danish, and Danish coins within the German sections.
This only teaches us that even history isn’t static. Who knows - in a hundred years, new research may have moved more coins 
from one country or one city to another. What we know today will certainly be questioned tomorrow, as it should. 
  
The Oldest Coin Type of Sweden 
The Very First Swedish Coin in the L. E. Bruun Collection
14121
SWEDEN. Penny, ND (ca. 995-1022). Sigtuna Mint. Olof Skötkonung. NGC AU-58. Delzanno-24; Llt-13; Malm-
er-4.17.56; SMH-30; Bruun-11139. Weight: 2.20 gms. Moneyer: Godwine. Bust right, holding scepter, ‘+OLAFA REX 
ANCOL ’ / Short Cross, ‘C-R-U-X’ in angles, ‘+GODWINE MOTAOIAS’ . An old, dusty toning covers the uncirculated 
surfaces of this the oldest coin type of Sweden. This is the very first Swedish coin in the Bruun Catalogue, and the im-
portance of its provenance cannot be overstated. 
The name of the moneyer, Godwine can also be found on the earliest Norwegian coinage, that of Olav Tryggvason, and also 
on the first Anglo-Saxons style coinage of Denmark, that of Sven Tveskæg (Sweyn Forkbeard).
€3,500-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: A. C. Mohr Collection (Copenhagen - 1847), Lot # 3970.
From the Eskilstuna Hoard, found 1833, 90 km due west of Stockholm (deposited after 1002 AD).

========== PAGE 251 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 225
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Marvelous Condition with Great Eye Appeal
Fully Legible and Extremely Rare Thus
14122
SWEDEN. Penny, ND (1022-1050). Sigtuna Mint. Anund Jakob. NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. Delzanno-97; 
Llt-1; Malmer-9.199.1202; SMH-604; Bruun-11150. Weight: 1.25 gms. Moneyer: Ulah. Imitation of Cnut the Great’s 
(and Ethelred’s) Long Cross Coinage. Bust left, ‘ ANVND REX IN’ / Long Cross, ‘VLAH IN SITVN’ . Excessively rare. 
Astounding condition with a natural, old toning over the uncirculated surfaces. The tiny die striations are perfectly pre-
served as are all the minute details. Three peck marks on the obverse are the only things to reveal this coin was ever in 
circulation and not taken straight from the mint to the ground to the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Son of Olof Skötkonung, the name ‘ Anund’ was given by the Swedes, as ‘Jakob’, a Christian name, was both too foreign and 
strange sounding.
€10,000-€15,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 252 ==========

Page 226 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Very Rare and Excellent For Type
14123
SWEDEN. Penny (Bracteate), ND (1167-1193). Sigtuna Mint(?). Knut Eriksson. NGC AU-50. Delzanno-4; Llt-
1A:1a; Frösell-8; Bruun Auction-80; Bruun-11159. Weight: 0.31 gms. Very rare and immensely well-preserved bracteate 
with a half length depiction of the king holding fleur de Lis scepter and either a ciborium of perhaps a model of a church. 
Surrounding him are three letters; I, V , and A, an abbreviation whose meaning is lost in time, for now.
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Reverend J. B. Sorterup Collection (Copenhagen - 1586), Lot # 419.

Impressive Condition for this Paper-Thin Emission
14124
SWEDEN. Penny (Bracteate), ND (1167-1193). Lödöse Mint. Knut Eriksson. NGC MS-62. Delzanno-28; Llt-
XI:A:1a; Frösell-16; Bruun Auction-29; Bruun-11169. Weight: 0.14 gms. ‘KANVTVS REX S’; This rare and impressively 
thin Mint State bracteate (0.14 gms!) imitates Henry II’s English Sterling’s with its central en-face portrait and the sword 
in the legend.
Lödöse, some 40 km up the Kungsälv River on the Swedish west coast, was for centuries the country’s only Western port.
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
14125
SWEDEN. 6 Penny / Abo, ND (1396-1439). Åbo Mint. Erik of Pommerania. NGC EF-45. Galster-201; Delzanno-315; 
Llt-9b; Frösell-200 var.; Bruun Auction-215; Bruun-11276. Weight: 0.79 gms. Excellent condition for type; fully legible 
with handsome surfaces. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 254 ==========

Page 228 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Excessively Rare Interregnum Issue
Far Surpassing the Few Other Known Specimens in Condition and Beauty
14126
SWEDEN. Örtug, ND (1465-1467). Stockholm Mint. Interregnum. NGC AU-55. Delzanno-369; Frösell-224; Llt-1; 
Bruun Auction-222; Bruun-11283. Weight: 1.42 gms. Excessively rare. Variation with large cross on obverse. A breath-
takingly beautiful example, lustrous and nearly ‘as struck’ . All three sub-types of the Interregnum coinage are very rare 
in any condition but impossibly so as well-preserved as this. The two examples sold in Sweden in 2009 and 2019 were 
either bent and brushed or spotted with planchet defects, putting this AU example in stark contrast. 
There were multiple interregnums in 15th and 16th century Sweden as noblemen and kings fought over the throne. This 
particular regency, lasting three years, saw three different regents.
€2,500-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 255 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 229
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Exquisite Condition with Fully Legible Legends
14127
SWEDEN. Örtug, ND (1457-1461). Stockholm Mint. Kristian I. NGC VF-35. Galster-214; Delzanno-365; Llt-1; Bru-
un Auction-254; Bruun-11316a. Weight: 1.32 gms. Excessively rare, this being the variation with ‘K’ on the obverse and 
no stars by its side. Fully legible with pleasing surfaces far above its numeric grade.
Christian I (of Denmark) was chosen by the nobility after they deposed Karl Knutsson Bonde. He quickly became unpopular 
after imposing heavy taxes, and a rebellion broke out in 1463. Karl Knutsson Bonde returned to the throne in 1464. This 
variation of Christian’s Stockholm coinage, with the uncial ‘K’ is believed to be the very first type.
€1,200-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 256 ==========

Page 230 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Excessively Rare Silver Emission of Exceptional Preservation
Possibly One of Only Two Known
14128
SWEDEN. Örtug, ND (1497-1501). Stockholm Mint. Johan II (Hans). NGC AU-50. Delzanno-424; Frösell-282; Llt-2; 
Bruun-11316q. Weight: 1.37 gms. Extremely rare variation with cross on obverse, possibly only one other known in private 
collections - this being the better example by far of the two. It is well-centered, well-struck, fully legible and has the most 
beautiful toning. It is a coin that will turn heads and attract serious bidders and one that should most certainly be seen in 
person.
Like his father Christian I, Johan III (Hans of Denmark) had only a short period as ruler of Sweden, as he was deposed in 
1501 after only four year on the throne. All his Swedish Coins are rarities.
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Johan G Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918).
    
14129
SWEDEN. Öre, 1529. Stockholm Mint. Gustav Vasa. NGC AU-50. SM-52(unlisted, cf. 47); Delzanno-188; Appelgren-
cf.191(497); Bruun-11419. Weight: 3.65 gms. Mintmaster: Herman Fossert. Extremely rare variation with ‘G’ in shield 
between the king’s feet and crossed arrows with ‘G’ underneath on the reverse. Despite the slightly weak central strike, 
a fully legible coin with sharp details and a substantial amount of luster, especially on the obverse. Exceptionally good 
condition for type. 
€1,200-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 257 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 231
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
14130
SWEDEN. 1/2 Mark, 1536. Stockholm Mint. Gustav Vasa. NGC EF Details—Cleaned. SM-132(124); Delzanno-103; 
Appelgren-518(1295); Bruun Auction-403; Bruun-11447. Weight: 6.59 gms. Very rare and excellent for type, 1536 was 
the introductory year for the Mark and Half Marks coins in Sweden. Minute green deposits in a few crevices (wax?) and 
while weakly struck at center, the portrait is still relatively clear. The rest of the coin is superb, the planchet is flat and 
uniform and the legend clear. 
€1,500-€2,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Gorgeous and Extremely Well-Preserved Taler  
at the Highest Artistic Level
14131
SWEDEN. Daler, 1550. Svartsjö Mint. Gustav Vasa. NGC AU-55. Dav-8698; SM-170a(163); Delzanno-29; Bruun 
Auction-379; Bruun-11490. Weight: 28.92 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Hansson. Engraver: Erik Olufsson. Among the most 
beautiful of the 16th century’s Swedish coins, with a both delicate and powerful portrait of the king on the obverse and 
of Christ on the reverse. That, and the very high numeric grade for type will surely make for fierce bidding, as this coin 
will be a jewel in any collection.
The Svartsjö Mint was only operational from 1540 to 1550.
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 258 ==========

Page 232 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
    
14132
SWEDEN. Daler, 1567/6. Stockholm Mint. Erik XIV . NGC VF-30. Dav-8702; SM-10(10); Delzanno-30; Bruun Auc-
tion-429; Bruun-11543. Weight: 28.60 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Höijer. Some flatness, most noticeably on the portrait, 
but fully legible. This emission is famous for its many variations but the (very clear) overdate is not mentioned in either 
of the SMs or Delzanno. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Incredibly Well-Preserved and Excessively Rare
14133
SWEDEN. Mark, 1593. Stockholm Mint. Sigismund. NGC MS-61. SM-10(8); Delzanno-10; Bruun-11647. Weight: 
7.59 gms. Extremely rare and in superb condition, ‘as struck’ with lustrous surfaces, razor sharp details and a natural, 
slightly mottled toning. In terms of condition and rarity one of the most impressive of the Swedish coins in this sale.
Sigismund III Vasa was the son of Johan III and grandson of Gustav Vasa. Upon the death of his father he had already inherited 
the throne in Catholic Poland from his maternal grandfather. The Swedish Nobility was not keen on having a Catholic king, but 
with the Uppsala Resolution of 1594, which secured Protestantism throughout Sweden, he was allowed to take the throne. A de-
vout Catholic, Sigismund blatantly disregarded the Resolution, however, and after a civil war he was officially deposed in 1599. 
He remained king of Poland and fought many wars till his death in 1632 - same year as his cousin, Gustav II Adolf of Sweden.
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 259 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 233
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Very Rare Taler from the Highly Independent  
Swedish County of Södermanland
14134
SWEDEN. Daler, 1594. Stockholm Mint. Karl (XI), Duke of Södermanland. NGC EF Details—Cleaned. Dav-8711; 
SM-11(11); Delzanno-7; Hagander-43; Bruun-11680. Weight: 28.75 gms. The Daler was the only denomination struck 
in Stockholm by Karl IX as a duke. Very rare, light tooling in fields but excellent for type.
€10,000-€20,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Leopold W . von Wellenheim Collection (Vienna - 1846), Lot # 10081.

Page 234 Stack’s Bowers Galleries

14135
SWEDEN. Mark Klippe, 1598. Karl (IX) Duke of Söderman -
land. NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. SM-16a(16a); Delzanno-12; 
Bruun Auction-656; Bruun-11684. Weight: 5.65 gms. Mintmas-
ter: Niels Guldsmed. Rare klippe, variation with full date. Well-
struck, centered and sharp. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
14136
SWEDEN. 4 Öre, 1603. Stockholm Mint. Karl (IX) Riks -
föreståndare (Regent). NGC AU-50. KM-10; SM-19(19); Del-
zanno-23; Bruun Auction-661; Bruun-11690. Weight: 4.62 gms. 
Mintmaster: Antoni Grooth. Very rare emission, this example is 
almost unbelievably well-preserved; a flat and round planchet, a 
clear and uniform strike and a grade of preservation which is far 
better than what one could possibly hope for. The numeric grade 
seems particularly harsh given the exemplary, original surfaces 
where the smallest imperfections in the dies are still visible, and 
the amount of luster, especially significant on the reverse. 
€1,500-€2,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
14137
SWEDEN. 16 Mark (Carolin), 1608. Stockholm Mint. Karl 
IX. NGC Unc Details—Bent. Fr-19; KM-25; SM-11(7); SG-1; 
Bruun-11709. Weight 4.97 gms. Mintmaster: Antoni Grooth. 
Struck on gold from Hungarian Guldens paid as import duty on 
goods entering Sweden. The type often displays waviness to the 
planchet and one cannot help but wonder if the grading is a little 
bit harsh on such a superbly well-preserved specimen, lustrous 
and well-struck. 
€8,000-€10,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Johan Sharp Collection (Stockholm - 1851/1853), Lot # 381.

tion-692; Bruun-11718. Weight: 1.66 gms. Mintmaster: Antoni 
Grooth. This very rare one-year type is both lustrous and well 
struck. Small filing mark on the edge at 12 o’ clock.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

14139
SWEDEN. Riksdaler (Salvatordaler), 1617. Stockholm Mint. 
Gustav II Adolf. NGC EF Details—Cleaned. Dav-4516; KM-

787; Bruun-11767. Weight: 30.98 gms. Mintmaster: Gillis Gillis-
son Coyet. A rare and highly sought emission, this Salvatordal -

handsome with pleasing toning. 
€2,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 261 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 235
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Handsome and Imposing Single-Y ear Taler
14140
SWEDEN. Elbing. Riksdaler, 1628. Elbing Mint. Gustav II Adolf. NGC AU Details—Obverse Spot Removed. Dav-
4565; KM-15; SB-13b; Delzanno-31; Hagander-96; Bruun auction-888; Bruun-11827. Weight 28.90 gms. Date on both 
sides. Mintmaster: Marsilius Philipsen. An impressive Riksdaler, with nice surfaces and only a minor imperfection as 
noted by NGC.
The Prussian city of Elbing (present day Poland) was under Swedish control 1626-1635 and again 1655-1660, and an exten-
sive minting started in 1628. The higher denominations, however, are all rare and highly sought, especially in as beautiful a 
condition as this. The City arms with the two crosses were intentionally made to resemble that of Danzig in order to facilitate 
the acceptance of the coinage in Poland.
€10,000-€15,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Fröling Collection (Stockholm - 1857), Lot # 246.

Highly Attractive, Near Uncirculated Livonian Taler
Exceptional Condition for Type
14141
SWEDEN. Swedish Livonia. Riga. Taler, 1629-MW . Riga Mint. Gustav II Adolf. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-
4587; KM-16; SB-4; Delzanno-414; Haljak-1454; Hagander-130; Bruun Auction-868; Bruun-11839. Weight: 28.78 gms. 
Mintmaster: Martin Wulff. The ‘cleaned’ designation seems a bit harsh on this coin, with its vibrant, lustrous surfaces, 
delicate die striations and pleasing yet subdued toning. Scarce as type but truly a unicorn in this condition.
€8,000-€10,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
14142
SWEDEN. Mainz. Taler, 1631. Mainz Mint. Gustav II Adolf. NGC EF-45. Dav-4544(Erfurt); KM-51(Erfurt); SB-
21C(Erfurt); Delzanno-373; Bruun Auction-930; Bruun-11857. Weight: 29.08 gms. Mintmaster: Daniel Ayrer. Previ -
ously listed under Erfurt, this Taler, which with smaller variations was also minted in, among others, Hildesheim and 
Erfurt, celebrates the Swedish / Protestant victory at Breitenfeld 1631 during the Thirty Y ears’ War. The mintmaster’s 
mark, the Astrological symbol for the planet Mercury, can be seen on the reverse under the date ‘MDCXXXI’ . 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Magnificent Taler with Plenty of Luster and Lots of Eye Appeal
Struck on Silver Looted from the German City of Würzburg in Bavaria
14143
SWEDEN. Würzburg. Taler, 1631. Würzburg(?) Mint. Gustav II Adolf. NGC AU-55. Dav-4559a; KM-90; SB-7b; 
Delzanno-449; Hagander-139; Bruun Auction-978; Bruun-11859. Weight: 29.00 gms. Mintmaster: Mathaeus Lauffer(?). 
Light toning in the peripheries and retaining most (nearly all) of its luster, this piece is in a league of its own, incredibly 
well-preserved, lots of eye appeal and gorgeous detailing.
After conquering Würzburg in 1631 gold and silver were plundered from the city churches and gold and silver coins struck, 
either in Würzburg itself or at the army camp outside the city. On some gold Ducats of the same design as this coin the ini-
tials ‘ML ’ can be seen on the King’s armor, but not on the Talers.
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Münzkabinett der Stadtbibliothek zu Leipzig / Leipzig City Library Collection (Leipzig - 1853), Lot #9593.

========== PAGE 264 ==========

Page 238 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Extremely Rare Double Ducat 
Possibly Only Five in Private Hands
14144
SWEDEN. Augsburg. 2 Ducats, 1632. Augsburg Mint. Gustav II Adolf. NGC AU Details—Obverse Scratched. Fr-
111; KM-69; SB-2; Sg-26; Hagander-86; Bruun Auction-907; Bruun-11870. Weight: 7.00 gms. Mintmaster: Balthasar 
Schmidt. While the obverse shows light signs of wear, including the discreet scratches, the reverse holds much luster. A 
slight waviness to the flan does not distract from the beauty of this extremely rare Double Ducat with only about five 
known in private collections.
Augsburg in Southern Bavaria was conquered by Swedish forces in 1632. They held it till 1634 when the Imperial army 
retook it, and coins in the name of Gustav II Adolf were minted in 1632, 1633, and 1634.
€10,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
14145
SWEDEN. Erfurt. Ducat, 1632-IS. Erfurt Mint. Gustav II Adolf. NGC Unc Details—Obverse Damage. Fr-919; KM-
63; SB-11a; SG-55; Hagander-cf. 99; Bruun Auction-920; Bruun-11873. Weight: 3.42 gms. Mintmaster: Johann Schnei-
der. An absolutely marvelous portrait adorns this Ducat; expertly cut dies and a precise strike shows off an abundance of 
wonderful details. The discreet mintmark hides in the obverse legend as a conjoined ‘IS’ just over the king’s head. A true 
gem regardless of the tiny nick (testmark) before the portrait. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Leopold W . von Wellenheim Collection (Vienna - 1846), Lot # 10110.

Magnificent Taler with a Sublime Rendering of Christ on the Reverse
14146
SWEDEN. Fürth. Taler, 1632-CS. Fürth Mint. Gustav II Adolf. NGC MS-62. Dav-4549; KM-9; SB-2; Delzanno-361; 
Bruun Auction-947; Bruun-11876. Weight: 28.77 gms. Mintmaster: Conrad Stutz. Rare Taler in excellent Mint State 
condition. A discreet number in ink ‘2626’ can be seen on the obverse: the number refers to Madai’s Vollständige Thaler-
Cabinett from 1765, which was for a long time used as a reference.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Excessively Rare Ducat with Very Few in Private Ownership
14147
SWEDEN. Mainz. 2 Ducats, 1632-HIA. Gustav II Adolf. NGC AU-55. Fr-1694a; KM-509; SB-2; SG-69: Hagander-cf. 
115; Bruun-11877. Weight 6.86 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Jakob Ayrer. Very rare with only a handful known in private 
hands (Delzanno lists one but has not taken the Bruun specimen into account). 
Struck in the year of Gustav Adolf ’s death on the battlefield at the battle of Lützen, four year into Sweden’s participation in 
the Thirty Years’ War.
€15,000-€20,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
14148
SWEDEN. Nürnberg. Ducat, 1632. Nürnberg Mint. Gustav II Adolf. NGC AU-55. Fr-1924; KM-120; SB-4; SG-85; 
Hagander-126; Bruun Auction-963; Bruun-11880. Weight: 3.47 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Christian Lauer. This rare one-
year type boasts lovely surfaces surpassing its numeric grade. The star in the obverse legend is the mintmaster’s mark. 
Remnants of red wax indicates this coin was possibly plated in a previous sale.
Nürnberg, or Nuremberg, was like so many other German cities conquered by the Swedish army in 1632. Ducats (including 
multiples) and Talers were minted in that year alone. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
14149
SWEDEN. Nürnberg. Taler, 1632. Nürnberg Mint. Gustav II 
Adolf. NGC AU-50. Dav-4550; KM-118; SB-5; Delzanno-380; 
Hagander-127; Bruun Auction-965; Bruun-11881. Weight: 29.04 
gms. Mintmaster: Hans Christian Lauer. Some luster hugs the

€1,000-€2,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Leopold W . von Wellenheim Collection (Vienna - 1846), Lot # 
10119.
    
14150
SWEDEN. Strassburg. Ducat, 1632. Strassburg Mint. Gustav 
II Adolf. NGC AU-55. Fr-3311(Stettin Mint); KM-unlisted; SB-
1(unknown mint); SG-89; Hagander-142(unknown mint); Bru-
un Auction-973; Bruun-11885. Weight: 3.44 gms. Variation with 
‘FINLN’ . Most appealing, with bright luster and sharp details.
Previously linked to Stettin (Szczecin), recent studies now indi -
cate this coinage was struck in Strassburg (French: Strasbourg) 
in 1633/34 as tribute to the Swedish army. The crossed pokers 
mintmark of the mintmaster of Strassburg, Johan Hermann, can 
be seen on the obverse under the shield. Truly a fascinating piece 
of history, this lustrous gold Ducat teaches us that discoveries still 
await us in numismatics. 
€2,500-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
14151
SWEDEN. 1/2 Taler, ND (1634). Stockholm or Nürnberg 
Mint. Gustav Vasa. NGC VF Details—Scratches. KM-unlisted; 
SM-108; SB-107; Hild-180; Delzanno-112; Bruun Auction-987; 
Bruun-11889. Very rare largesse coinage struck in 1634 in either 
Nuremberg or Stockholm on the funeral of Gustav II Adolf. Pre-
viously believed to be a medal and listed by Hildebrand thus. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: J. Siökrona Collection (Stockholm - 1883).
    
14152
SWEDEN. Wolgast. 1/4 Taler, 1633. Wolgast Mint. Gustav II 
Adolf. NGC EF-40. KM-69; SB-44(Erfurt); Delzanno-446; Bru-
un Auction-945; Bruun-11901. Weight: 7.07 gms. Mintmaster: 
Johan Schneider (“Weissmantel”). Possibly attributed to Erfurt. 
The obverse image of a vine growing out of a skull and cross-
bones is a symbol for life defeating death. Planchet flaws at rim, 
excellent original toning.
Johan Schneider worked in Erfurt, and for years the coin was be-
lieved to have been struck there. Recent studies indicate, however, 
that the dies were cut in Erfurt, taken to Wolgast, where they were 
used to strike largesse money for the king’s funeral procession. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Acquired at auction in Amsterdam, 1852, Lot # 155.

Page 242 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II
Highly Attractive and Alluring Taler Klippe
Top Pop Example
14153
GERMANY . Osnabrück. Taler Klippe, 1633. Osnabrück Mint. Franz Wilhelm von Wartenberg. NGC AU-53. Dav-
unlisted; KM-33; Bruun-11903. Weight: 29.08 gms. Top Pop example of this rare siege klippe, retaining a fair bit of luster.
Struck during the siege of Osnabrück by the Swedish army under the command of Axel Oxenstierna and Gustav Horn. The 
city changed from Catholic to Protestant hands several time during the Thirty Years’ War and would at the Westphalian 
Peace in 1648 be recognized as bi-confessional Lutheran and Catholic. While this is a German issue we retain the Bruun 
order of coins in the sales.
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
14154
SWEDEN. Erfurt. Ducat, 1634. Erfurt Mint. Gustav II Adolf. NGC MS-60. Fr-923; KM-54; SB-15a; SG-cf. 55ff (for 
obverse).; Hagander-cf. 108 (for obverse); Bruun Auction-923; Bruun-11909. Weight: 3.42 gms. Mintmaster: Johann 
Schneider. These posthumous strikes (Gustav II Adolf died in 1632) have been subject to intense studies (Ottar Ertzheid 
Collection in particular), yet none appear to have listed this variation; the obverse is identical to the 1634, SG-55ff, hav-
ing a distinct, crooked leaf or flower in the legend, but is married to a completely different reverse. The reverse of this 
coin is similar in type to SG-48-49 but has a horseshoe in the legend at 12 o’ clock. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: J. Siökrona Collection (Stockholm - 1883).

Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
14155
SWEDEN. Stralsund. Ducat, 1638-CS. Stralsund Mint. Kristina. NGC AU Details—Edge Filing. Fr-3368; KM-102; 
SB-1; SG-53; Hagander-194; Bruun Auction-1153; Bruun-11943. Weight: 3.41 gms. Mintmaster: Casper Sievers. Lightly 
bent, this coin is well-struck with very pleasing surfaces and sharp details. As a curiosity it is dated on both sides.
Having had minting right since the 13th century, Stralsund issued this Ducat after Swedish conquest of the city, bearing 
the arms of the city on the obverse (an arrow or spearhead) and the name and symbol of the Holy Roman Emperor on the 
reverse, but no mention of the Swedish rulers - much to the Swedes’ dismay. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

faces and spectacular detailing. An interesting die correction is in the date, where an underlying ‘04’ is seen as a shadow 
under the ‘40’ .

========== PAGE 275 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 249
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
14167
SWEDEN. Bremen & Verden. 4 Mark, 1660-MM. Stade Mint. Karl X Gustav. NGC AU Details—Scratches. Dav-
321; KM-61; SB-7a; Delzanno-78; Bruun Auction-1223; Bruun-12130. Weight: 21.77 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Möller. 
Excellent old toning on this scarce and historically interesting one-year type.
The Bishopric of Bremen & Verden were ceded to Sweden at the Westphalian Peace in 1648 and turned into a hereditary 
dukedom. Since the Hanseatic city of Bremen itself was separate from the bishopric, the Bishopric Mint was placed in the 
town of Stade. In 1712 the now dukedom was conquered by the Danes and in 1719 ceded to the Duke of Hanover (better 
known as George I of Great Britain). 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: August Chr. Mohr Collection (Copenhagen - 1847), Lot # 4399.
Fine Ducat with a Charming Portrait of the 10-Y ear-Old King
14168
SWEDEN. Ducat, 1666. Stockholm Mint. Karl XI. NGC AU-55. Fr-42; KM-256; SM-10(8); SG-10; Hagander-233; 
Bruun Auction-1225; Bruun-12195. Weight: 3.34 gms. Mintmaster: Abraham Koch. Engraver: Johan Georg Breuer. 
Slightly bent, retaining a good deal of luster. 
Struck under the regency of his mother, Queen Hedvig Eleonora, as his father and predecessor, King Karl X Gustav, had died 
in 1660 when Karl XI was just four years old.
€10,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

14169
SWEDEN. Pommerania. Ducat, 1666-HIH. Szczecin Mint. Karl XI. NGC Unc Details—Obverse Graffiti. Fr-2116;

Johann Hille. Extremely rare Ducat with less than a handful known in private collections, well-struck and fully lustrous.
Szczecin or Stettin, on the Oder River just 40 miles from the Baltic Sea, lies on the Polish/German border and has held 
strategic importance for centuries. 
€6,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

14170

Hagander-302; Bruun Auction-1647; Bruun-12310; Weight: 28.89 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Ridder. This is most likely the 
finest example to change hands in more than 20 years. Quite a lot of luster hugs the devices and old, fine toning covers 
the surfaces.
Struck in the name of the Holy Roman Emperor, Leopold I, it also carries the image of St. Laurentius, or Lawrence, Wismar’s 
patron saint, who is often depicted holding the gridiron upon which he was martyred and killed in 258 AD.

Scarce One-Y ear Type with Pleasing Surfaces
14174
SWEDEN. Stade (Lower Saxony). Taler, 1686-IS. Stade Mint. Karl XI. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-5814; KM-
27; SB-2a; Delzanno-713; Hagander-298; Bruun Auction-1530; Bruun-12442. Weight: 28.77 gms. Mintmaster: Jacob 
Schröder. A very rare one-year type, this coin exhibits excellent eye-appeal with a lightly golden hue.
The Hanseatic City of Stade fell under Swedish rule in 1648. While no official Swedish coinage was produced there, the city 
itself did on two occasions mint silver coins: Firstly in 1676,when Schillings and Sechslings were minted, at a time when it 
was occupied by enemy troops; and secondly in 1686, when the city itself minted Double and Single Talers. The issuing of 
coins in the name of the Holy Roman Emperor did not please the Swedish king (although several other cities did precisely 
that, Stralsund and Wismar, for example), and the minting was quickly stopped, further adding to the scarcity.
€7,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
14175
SWEDEN. Pomerania. 2 Ducats, 1696-ICA. Szczecin Mint. Karl XI. NGC Unc Details—Obverse Damage. Fr-2117; 
KM-342; SB-59; SG-83; Bruun Auction-1533; Bruun-12543. Weight: 6.94 gms. Mintmaster: Julius Christian Arensburg. 
Slightly wavy but lustrous, the reverse showing the Swedish royal regalia atop a sheaf in front of a city view of Szczecin/
Stettin. 
€8,000-€10,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Visually Compelling Ducat with Charming Imagery  
and Attractive Surfaces
14185
SWEDEN. Wismar. Ducat, 1743. Lübeck Mint. Fredrik I. NGC MS-63. Fr-3530; KM-127; SB-76a; SG-75; Hagan -
der-430; Bruun-12891. Weight: 3.44 gms. Mintmaster: Caspar August Falk. Engraver: Johann Friedrich Rahm. A touch 
of striking weakness at the rim as common for type, this large gold emission shows soft toning over the enigmatic im-
agery: the city’s crest, a bull and the striped flag, over which an armored arm with sword; on the reverse the Imperial 
Double Eagle of the Holy Roman Empire. A powerful, visually compelling coin.
There is little consensus as to where this issue was actually struck and who the mintmaster was. Some argue that the ar -
moured arm was the mintmark of Hans Ridder, who was mintmaster in Wismar 1670-1674, and that the mintmark was 
also used by his son, Heinrich Ridder, mintmaster in Lübeck at the time of minting. However, it is more likely the coin was 
struck in Wismar itself, by the mintmaster and engraver mentioned above, and that the armoured arm is simply copied from 
the older coinage without knowing the meaning thereof.
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 289 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 263
Session II • Saturday , March 15, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
COINS OF GERMANY
THE DUCHIES OF SCHLESWIG AND HOLSTEIN
The border between Schleswig and Holstein was, at least since the 9th cen-
tury, the border between (what is now) Denmark and its southern neigh-
bors. The mighty Danevirke, an over 30 km long earthen mound with for-
tifications, which stands as a testament to the importance of the region, has 
recently been proved to be as old as the 5th century. However, those are the 
Dark Ages, and we have little to no written sources until around the early 
9th century, so we begin when the bright light of history starts illuminating 
our path.
Those early written sources tell of Charlemagne fighting the Danes at Dan-
evirke and even having his elephant brought up to scare the Danes. The 
elephant was a gift from Harun al-Rashid, the Caliph in Baghdad. Unfor -
tunately, the elephant died of pneumonia on its way north. They also tell of 
King Godfred, who razed the merchant harbour of Rerik and moved the 
merchants by force to the newly founded city of Haithabu (Hedeby), just 
north of the Danevirke, to ensure his control of the market.
Over the centuries, protecting the southern border became increasingly 
important and the Danish monarch needed a strong presence in the guise 
of earls and dukes. The strong dukes were, however, a double-edged sword; 
keeping them on the side of the Danish kings proved difficult as they formed 
alliances across the border with the dukes of Holstein. 
In the late 15th century the old Danish royal main line died out, and from 
a branch of this lineage, the House of Oldenburg, Christian I was elected 
king. During the 16th century his descendants would fight for the throne, 
and some coins struck by dukes, and later kings of Denmark are now regarded as part of Danish monetary history. As we 
have chosen to keep the 1928 Bruun catalog order in this auction, you will therefore find coins headlined “Denmark” under 
the “Germany” section.
From the 16th century the king of Denmark was also duke of both Schleswig and Holstein but had to share the lands with 
numerous lesser dukes. Due to power struggles, marriages and inheritances, the two duchies were a patchwork of divisions, 
sub-divisions and sub-sub-divisions, many of which struck their own coinage.
The largest holders of territory were the Gottorp line and the Sonderburg line. The cities of Lübeck and Hamburg were 
autonomous cities, but there was also a Prince-Bishopric of Lübeck which had its own lands and its own currency indepen-
dent of that of the City of Lübeck.
Borders changed constantly as wars were fought, and lines died out and were incorporated into another family line or were 
divided between siblings. Larger military powers also interfered, both militarily and matrimonially. The Gottorp line, for 
instance, married within the Swedish royal line creating an uncomfortable geopolitical situation for Denmark with its arch 
enemy on both its northeastern and its southern borders. But all the royal and ducal lines were related by marriage then. 
For example, Friederich IV of Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp, who ruled from 1695 to 1702, was the grandson of Frederik III 
of Denmark. He married the sister of the Swedish king, Karl XII, who was also the grandson of Frederik III of Denmark. 
Friederich IV fathered a son with his Swedish princess, a son who married the daughter of Peter the Great of Russia. His 
son, the grandson of Friedrich IV , became Tsar of Russia as Peter III, the Tsar who ruled for only six months before he was 
murdered on the orders of his wife, Catherine the Great.
Marrying within the royal lines may have been an act of diplomacy but it certainly didn’t stop families from waging war on 
each other, and the geopolitical outline of Schleswig-Holstein was as confusing as its ducal lineages.
In the 19th century, two wars of independence were fought against the Danes. The first, 1848-1851, ended with a Danish 
win, but in the Second Schleswig War Denmark lost, as both Prussia and the Austro-Hungarian Empire sided against it. 
Losing the war in 1864 also meant a loss of a large part of the Jutland Peninsula. In 1920, After the First World War, a ref-
erendum was made on where the duchies wanted to belong, and a new border was drawn based on the votes. This border 
between Denmark and Germany, which brought Northern Schleswig back to Denmark, has stood for over 100 years now, 
with minorities living peacefully on both sides of it.


========== PAGE 290 ==========

Page 264 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part II

14202
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1606-MP . Lübeck Mint. Johann Adolf. NGC 
AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-5438; KM-26.1; Lange-268a cf.; Sieg-9.9; Bruun-14116. Weight: 28.83 gms. Rare. Variation 
with ‘Co OL ‧ E ‧ DE ‧’ . Mintmaster: Matthias Puls. Minted by Johann Adolf as Bishop of Lübeck. Appealing and pre-
sentable, with limited wear and much charm despite the cleaning.
€2,500-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Leopold Welzl v. Wellenheim Collection (Vienna - 1847) Lot # 5135.

Double Taler of the Utmost Beauty and Rarity
Wonderful and Crisp Surfaces
    
14203
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. 2 Taler, 1607-IG. Steinbeck Mint. Johann Adolf. NGC AU-55. Dav-LS439; 
KM-cf.17; Sieg-12.1; Lange-cf. 264a; Bruun-14118. Mintmaster: Jonas Georgens. This extremely rare and wonderful 
Double Taler (Lange only had galvanic copies of the types, and not even that of this variation!) has some light strike dou-
bling which is to be expected on the large silver issues. The highly detailed and powerful yet romantic double portrait, 
the wonderful steely grey silver color with hints of warm toning and plenty of luster makes this a sublime combination 
of beauty and rarity.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: H. M. Mayntzhusen Collection (Copenhagen - 1883).

14212
